questions.prompt: Vector quantity describing the straight-line change in position from start to end, with direction.
questions.explanation: Displacement is defined as the vector from initial to final position: d = x_f - x_i. Unlike distance, which is a scalar path length, displacement carries direction and can be zero or negative even when distance is large. A runner who completes one lap of a 400-m track has traveled 400 m of distance but has zero displacement because the start and finish positions coincide. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list d as the displacement variable in the kinematics equations and require vector treatment in projectile and two-dimensional problems. On released Regents items, students must distinguish displacement from distance when reading position-time graphs (slope of x vs t gives velocity, not displacement). Galileo Galilei first analyzed accelerated motion rigorously in the early 1600s, laying the groundwork that Newton formalized in the 1687 Principia.

questions.prompt: Scalar quantity equal to the total path length traveled, regardless of direction.
questions.explanation: Distance is the cumulative length of the path an object follows, with no directional information. A car driving 5 km north then 5 km south has traveled 10 km of distance but has a displacement of zero. The SI unit is the meter. Distance is always non-negative and can never decrease. On Regents items, distance vs displacement is a classic distractor pair: students who confuse them will incorrectly use the path length where the vector displacement is required in v = d/t for average velocity, which must use displacement (with direction). Galileo's inclined plane experiments in the early 1600s measured distances rolled per unit time, establishing the foundational kinematic relationship d proportional to t^2 for uniform acceleration from rest.

questions.prompt: Vector rate of change of displacement, including both speed and direction.
questions.explanation: Velocity is defined as v = d/t where d is displacement (vector), giving SI units of m/s with a specified direction. Average velocity over an interval uses total displacement divided by total time; instantaneous velocity is the limit of this ratio as time goes to zero, equivalent to the slope of a position-time graph at a single point. A car moving 25 m/s east has a different velocity than one moving 25 m/s west, even though their speeds are equal. The NYS Reference Tables provide v_f = v_i + at and v_avg = (v_i + v_f)/2 for uniformly accelerated motion. Galileo distinguished average from instantaneous velocity in his 1638 Two New Sciences, and Newton formalized velocity as a vector derivative in his calculus-based mechanics.

questions.prompt: Scalar magnitude of velocity, describing how fast an object moves regardless of direction.
questions.explanation: Speed is the magnitude of the velocity vector and equals distance divided by time, with SI units of m/s. Unlike velocity, speed has no direction and is always non-negative. A car traveling around a circular track at constant 20 m/s has constant speed but continuously changing velocity (direction changes), and therefore experiences centripetal acceleration. On the Regents, students must recognize that an object moving in a circle at uniform speed is still accelerating because velocity is a vector. The speedometer in a car reads speed, not velocity. Average speed = total distance / total time, which differs from average velocity (displacement / time) whenever the path is not a straight line. This scalar-vector distinction is heavily tested in projectile and circular-motion items.

questions.prompt: Vector rate of change of velocity per unit time, measured in m/s squared.
questions.explanation: Acceleration is defined as a = (v_f - v_i)/t, the change in velocity divided by the time interval. SI units are m/s^2. Acceleration occurs whenever velocity changes in magnitude, direction, or both — so an object moving in a circle at constant speed is accelerating (centripetal acceleration toward the center). Galileo first showed that freely falling objects near Earth accelerate uniformly at approximately 9.81 m/s^2 (the NYS Reference Tables list g = 9.81 m/s^2). Newton's second law, F = ma, ties acceleration directly to net force and inertial mass. On Regents items, students must distinguish positive acceleration (speeding up in the positive direction OR slowing down in the negative direction) from negative acceleration, and read acceleration as the slope of a velocity-time graph.

questions.prompt: Plot of object location versus time, whose slope at any point gives the instantaneous velocity.
questions.explanation: A position-time (x-t) graph plots an object's position on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis. The slope dx/dt at any instant equals the instantaneous velocity, and the slope of a secant line between two points gives the average velocity. A horizontal line indicates an object at rest; a straight tilted line indicates constant velocity; a curve indicates acceleration. A parabola opening upward represents uniform positive acceleration (as Galileo first demonstrated for free fall in his inclined plane experiments). On Regents items, students must read slope correctly (with units of m/s), identify when an object reverses direction (where slope changes sign), and distinguish position graphs from velocity graphs — a common distractor. The shape of an x-t graph encodes the full motion story without requiring algebra.

questions.prompt: Plot of velocity versus time, whose slope gives acceleration and whose area gives displacement.
questions.explanation: On a velocity-time (v-t) graph, the slope dv/dt at any point equals instantaneous acceleration, and the area under the curve between two times equals the displacement over that interval (positive area = positive displacement; negative area = displacement in the opposite direction). A horizontal v-t line means constant velocity (zero acceleration); a straight tilted line means uniform acceleration. The NYS Reference Tables kinematic equations all derive geometrically from v-t graph analysis. On Regents items, students are routinely asked to compute displacement as the trapezoidal area under a v-t graph or to identify acceleration from the slope. A common distractor confuses the y-intercept (initial velocity) with the slope (acceleration). Galileo's geometric reasoning in the 1638 Two New Sciences is the historical origin of this technique.

questions.prompt: Rise-over-run of a graph line, whose physical meaning depends on what each axis represents.
questions.explanation: Slope on a motion graph carries a specific physical meaning based on the axes: on a position-time graph the slope equals velocity (m/s); on a velocity-time graph the slope equals acceleration (m/s^2); on a force-displacement graph the slope is the spring constant (N/m); on a current-voltage graph for an ohmic resistor, slope equals 1/R (conductance). Students must always check axis units before interpreting slope. On the NYS Reference Tables, many fundamental relationships (F = kx, V = IR, p = mv) appear as straight lines whose slopes equal a physical constant. A Regents item showing a non-linear position-time graph requires students to recognize that the instantaneous slope (tangent line) gives velocity, while average slope (secant line) gives average velocity over the chosen interval.

questions.prompt: Region between the curve and the time axis, whose meaning depends on what is plotted vertically.
questions.explanation: The area under a graph encodes an accumulated quantity that depends on the vertical axis: area under a velocity-time graph equals displacement (m); area under an acceleration-time graph equals change in velocity (m/s); area under a force-time graph equals impulse (N·s = kg·m/s); area under a force-displacement graph equals work (J); area under a power-time graph equals energy delivered (J). On Regents items, students compute these as triangles, rectangles, or trapezoids from gridded data. A common distractor confuses area (displacement) with slope (acceleration) on a v-t graph. The integral interpretation underlies the kinematic equation d = v_i·t + (1/2)at^2, which is geometrically the area under a linearly increasing v-t line — a result Galileo derived geometrically before Newton's calculus.

questions.prompt: Velocity at a single moment in time, equal to the tangent-line slope on a position-time graph.
questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the limit of average velocity as the time interval approaches zero — v(t) = dx/dt — and it gives the velocity at one specific instant rather than averaged over an interval. Graphically, it is the slope of the tangent line at one point on a position-time graph. A speedometer reads instantaneous speed (the magnitude of instantaneous velocity). On a curved x-t graph, average velocity between two points (slope of secant line) generally differs from the instantaneous velocity at either endpoint. On Regents items, students must distinguish the two: a parabolic x-t graph has an instantaneous velocity that increases with time, but an average velocity over [0,t] equal to half the final value if starting from rest. Newton's calculus, developed in the 1660s, made this limit rigorous.

questions.prompt: Velocity at the start of an interval, denoted v_i in the NYS Reference Tables kinematics equations.
questions.explanation: Initial velocity (v_i) appears in every NYS Physics Reference Tables kinematic equation: v_f = v_i + at, d = v_i·t + (1/2)at^2, and v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2ad. It is a vector with both magnitude and direction; in 1D problems a sign convention (e.g., up positive, down negative) must be chosen and applied consistently. For an object dropped from rest, v_i = 0. For an object thrown upward, v_i > 0 if up is positive, and the same object will have v_f = 0 at the peak (then v_f negative on the way down). On Regents items, students often misidentify v_i when an object is decelerating: they must use the velocity at the start of the chosen interval, not the velocity at the start of the whole problem. Vectors require careful sign tracking, especially in two-stage problems.

questions.prompt: Velocity at the end of an interval, denoted v_f in the NYS Reference Tables kinematics equations.
questions.explanation: Final velocity (v_f) is the velocity at the end of the chosen time interval and appears in v_f = v_i + at and v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2ad on the NYS Reference Tables. For an object in free fall starting from rest, v_f = -gt (down negative). At the peak of a vertical throw, v_f = 0, even though the object is still accelerating downward at g = 9.81 m/s^2. Sign conventions are critical: a ball thrown up at 20 m/s reaches v_f = 0 at the peak, then has v_f = -20 m/s back at its launch height (same speed, opposite direction). Galileo first noted this symmetry in the 1638 Two New Sciences. On Regents items, students using v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2ad must take the appropriate square root sign — the equation gives the magnitude, and direction must be argued from the physics.

questions.prompt: Duration over which motion is analyzed, denoted t in the NYS Reference Tables kinematics equations.
questions.explanation: Time interval t = t_f - t_i represents the duration over which displacement, velocity change, or acceleration is being measured. SI unit is the second. In the kinematics equation d = v_i·t + (1/2)at^2, t is the elapsed time from when v_i was the velocity, not the absolute clock time. A common Regents trap is asking for the time to fall from rest through a given distance, requiring d = (1/2)gt^2 solved for t = sqrt(2d/g). For a vertical throw, the up-time equals the down-time to the same height (Galilean symmetry), so total flight time is twice the time to reach peak: t_total = 2·v_i/g. Students must read the problem carefully to identify which segment of motion the requested time refers to.

questions.prompt: Constant rate of velocity change with respect to time, the assumption underlying NYS Reference Tables kinematics equations.
questions.explanation: Uniform (constant) acceleration means a is the same at every instant during the interval — the kinematics equations on the NYS Physics Reference Tables (v_f = v_i + at, d = v_i·t + (1/2)at^2, v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2ad) apply only under this assumption. The most common uniform acceleration on the Regents is free-fall near Earth's surface, g = 9.81 m/s^2 downward, valid when air resistance is negligible. On a velocity-time graph, uniform acceleration appears as a straight line; on a position-time graph, as a parabola. Non-uniform acceleration (e.g., air drag, varying force) requires calculus and is beyond Regents scope. Galileo's inclined plane experiments demonstrated uniform acceleration empirically; Newton's second law F = ma explains it (constant net force produces constant acceleration on a constant mass).

questions.prompt: Motion under gravity alone, with all objects accelerating at g = 9.81 m/s squared regardless of mass.
questions.explanation: Free fall is motion governed solely by gravity, with no air resistance. Near Earth's surface, the NYS Reference Tables give g = 9.81 m/s^2 directed toward Earth's center (downward). A foundational result, demonstrated by Galileo from the Leaning Tower of Pisa (legend) and rigorously in the 1638 Two New Sciences: all objects fall with the same acceleration regardless of mass, because gravitational force F_g = mg and Newton's second law F = ma give a = g, independent of m. Apollo 15 astronaut David Scott famously demonstrated this on the Moon in 1971 by dropping a hammer and feather together. On Regents items, students apply the standard kinematics equations with a = -g (taking up as positive), recognize that an object thrown upward has v = 0 at the peak but still accelerates at g downward, and compute fall times and impact speeds for vertical drops.

questions.prompt: Two-dimensional motion of an object under gravity alone after being launched, with independent horizontal and vertical components.
questions.explanation: Projectile motion describes an object launched into the air and moving under gravity only (air resistance neglected). The defining principle, first articulated by Galileo in the 1638 Two New Sciences, is that horizontal and vertical motion are independent: horizontal velocity remains constant (no horizontal force), while vertical motion undergoes uniform downward acceleration g = 9.81 m/s^2. This produces a parabolic trajectory. A bullet fired horizontally hits the ground at the same time as one simply dropped from the same height. On Regents items, students decompose the initial velocity into v_ix = v_i·cos(theta) and v_iy = v_i·sin(theta), then apply kinematic equations separately to each axis. Maximum range on level ground occurs at launch angle 45° (with no air resistance), and equal ranges occur for complementary angles (e.g., 30° and 60°).

questions.prompt: Constant horizontal velocity v_x = v cos theta in projectile motion, unchanged by gravity.
questions.explanation: The horizontal component of a projectile's velocity, v_x = v·cos(theta), remains constant throughout the flight because no horizontal force acts on the projectile (neglecting air resistance). This is Galileo's principle of independence of motion (1638) and a direct consequence of Newton's first law: with zero horizontal net force, horizontal velocity does not change. Horizontal displacement is therefore x = v_x·t = v·cos(theta)·t. On Regents items, a common distractor adds a fictitious horizontal acceleration, which would only apply if there were a horizontal force (e.g., wind, thrust). For a horizontally launched projectile (theta = 0), v_x equals the entire launch speed, and the projectile travels x = v·t horizontally while falling y = (1/2)gt^2 vertically — the two combined trace a parabola.

questions.prompt: Vertical velocity v_y that changes uniformly due to gravity, with v_y = v sin theta - g t in projectile motion.
questions.explanation: The vertical component of a projectile's velocity changes uniformly under gravity: v_y(t) = v_iy - g·t, with v_iy = v·sin(theta) at launch (taking up as positive). At the peak of the trajectory, v_y = 0 (vertical velocity is momentarily zero), even though horizontal velocity v_x and acceleration g are unchanged. The time to peak is t_peak = v_iy/g, and the maximum height above launch is h_max = v_iy^2/(2g). On Regents items, students often confuse v_y at the peak (zero) with the speed at the peak (which equals v_x, not zero). Vertical and horizontal motions share only one variable: time t. To find range, students compute total flight time from vertical kinematics, then multiply by v_x to get horizontal distance traveled.

questions.prompt: Horizontal displacement of a projectile from launch to landing, maximized at 45 degrees on level ground.
questions.explanation: Range (R) is the horizontal distance traveled by a projectile from launch to landing. For a projectile launched and landing at the same height with initial speed v and angle theta on level ground (no air resistance), R = (v^2 sin(2theta))/g. This expression is maximized when sin(2theta) = 1, i.e., theta = 45°. Complementary launch angles (e.g., 30° and 60°) give equal ranges because sin(60°) = sin(120°). On Regents items, students more often compute range from t_total = 2v·sin(theta)/g, then R = v·cos(theta)·t_total. For a horizontally launched projectile (theta = 0), the simpler formula does not apply — students must use t = sqrt(2h/g) for the fall time, then R = v_x·t. Galileo's parabolic trajectory result (1638) is the foundation; Newton's mechanics generalizes it to any inverse-square gravitational field.

questions.prompt: Curved path of a projectile, a parabola in the absence of air resistance.
questions.explanation: The trajectory of a projectile is the curved path it follows through space. Under gravity alone (air resistance neglected), Galileo proved in the 1638 Two New Sciences that this path is a parabola. The trajectory equation, obtained by eliminating t from x = v_x·t and y = v_iy·t - (1/2)g·t^2, is y = (tan theta)x - [g/(2 v^2 cos^2 theta)] x^2 — a quadratic in x, confirming a parabola. The shape depends on the launch angle and speed but not on the projectile's mass (a key consequence of the equivalence between gravitational and inertial mass). On Regents items, students must recognize that at any point on the trajectory, the velocity is tangent to the parabola, with horizontal component constant and vertical component changing by -g·t. Real-world trajectories (baseballs, artillery shells) deviate from parabolas due to air drag.

questions.prompt: Coordinate system from which motion is observed and measured.
questions.explanation: A reference frame is the perspective (origin, axes, clock) from which an observer measures position, velocity, and acceleration. The same motion can have very different descriptions in different frames: a passenger in a moving train is at rest in the train's frame but moving relative to the ground. Newton's first law defines inertial reference frames as those in which an object with zero net force has zero acceleration. The Earth's surface is approximately inertial for most Regents problems (rotation effects negligible at short scales). Galilean relativity (1632) asserts that the laws of mechanics are identical in all inertial frames moving at constant velocity relative to one another — a principle Einstein extended in his 1905 special relativity to include electromagnetism and to require constant speed of light. On Regents items, students must specify the frame when stating velocity (e.g., 'relative to the ground' vs 'relative to the water').

questions.prompt: Physical quantity described fully by magnitude alone, with no direction.
questions.explanation: A scalar is a physical quantity that is fully specified by its magnitude (a number with units). Examples include mass, time, temperature, distance, speed, energy, work, power, electric potential, and charge. Scalars combine by ordinary algebraic addition (5 kg + 3 kg = 8 kg). Compare to vectors, which require both magnitude and direction. On the NYS Reference Tables, scalar quantities are typeset in plain (non-bold) variables. A Regents item asking for total energy adds joules arithmetically; one asking for total displacement requires vector addition because displacement is a vector. Common student confusions: temperature is a scalar (no direction), but heat transfer can be assigned a direction in problem-setting; mass is scalar, but weight is a force vector. Distinguishing scalars from vectors is foundational for projectile, momentum, and electric-field problems.

questions.prompt: Physical quantity that requires both magnitude and direction for complete specification.
questions.explanation: A vector is a physical quantity specified by both magnitude and direction. Examples include displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, momentum, impulse, electric field, and magnetic field. Vectors are added geometrically using the head-to-tail or parallelogram method, or algebraically by decomposing into perpendicular components (typically x and y). On the NYS Reference Tables, vectors are typeset in bold or with arrow overlines. Two vectors of equal magnitude can sum to zero (if antiparallel) or to twice their magnitude (if parallel). On Regents items, students must use vector addition (not arithmetic) when combining forces, velocities, or fields. The magnitude of a resultant vector formed by two perpendicular components a and b is sqrt(a^2 + b^2), with direction given by arctan(b/a). Vector reasoning underlies free-body diagrams, projectile motion, and Coulomb-force superposition.

questions.prompt: Single vector representing the sum of two or more vectors, found by head-to-tail or component addition.
questions.explanation: The resultant is the single vector equivalent to the sum of two or more vectors. Two perpendicular vectors of magnitudes A and B have a resultant of magnitude sqrt(A^2 + B^2) at angle arctan(B/A) from A. For non-perpendicular vectors, students decompose each into x and y components, sum the components separately (R_x = sum of A_x; R_y = sum of A_y), then combine: |R| = sqrt(R_x^2 + R_y^2). On Regents items, common resultant problems include: combining displacement vectors (a person walks 4 m east then 3 m north — resultant 5 m at 37°); finding net force on an object pulled by two ropes; computing total velocity of a boat crossing a current (velocity of boat plus velocity of water); and finding net electric field from two point charges. The resultant has the same physical effect as the sum of the individual vectors.

questions.prompt: Choice of positive and negative directions for vector components, applied consistently throughout a problem.
questions.explanation: A sign convention assigns positive and negative values to vector components based on a chosen positive direction (e.g., up positive, down negative; right positive, left negative; or east positive). All quantities in a 1D problem (displacement, velocity, acceleration, force) must be signed consistently with this convention. For vertical motion, the standard convention takes up as positive, making g = -9.81 m/s^2 (acceleration is downward, hence negative). A ball thrown upward at 20 m/s has v_i = +20 m/s; at the peak v = 0; back at launch height v = -20 m/s. On Regents items, sign errors are a leading cause of wrong answers in kinematics, especially in two-stage problems (e.g., dropped object hitting a moving floor) and in problems combining gravitational and applied forces. Establishing the sign convention at the start of a problem and labeling it explicitly is essential discipline.

questions.prompt: Vector push or pull on an object, measured in newtons, that can change the object's motion.
questions.explanation: Force is a vector interaction between objects that can change motion (accelerate, decelerate, redirect) or deform an object. The SI unit is the newton (N), defined as 1 kg·m/s^2 — the force required to accelerate a 1 kg mass at 1 m/s^2. Newton's three laws (Principia, 1687) define and quantify force: (1) an object's velocity changes only when a net force acts on it; (2) F_net = ma; (3) every force has an equal and opposite reaction. Forces always arise from interactions between two objects: gravitational (Earth on book), normal (table on book), tension (rope on box), friction (floor on shoe), spring (Hooke), electric (Coulomb), magnetic (current-carrying wires). On the Regents, students draw free-body diagrams listing all forces on a single object, then apply F_net = ma component-wise.

questions.prompt: Vector sum of all forces acting on an object, which equals mass times acceleration.
questions.explanation: The net force (F_net) is the vector sum of every individual force acting on an object: F_net = F_1 + F_2 + ... . By Newton's second law, F_net = ma, so an object accelerates in the direction of F_net with magnitude a = F_net/m. When F_net = 0, the object has zero acceleration and either remains at rest or moves at constant velocity (Newton's first law — equilibrium). On Regents items, students draw a free-body diagram, decompose each force into x and y components, sum each component separately, then apply F_net,x = m·a_x and F_net,y = m·a_y. A common distractor confuses net force with the largest individual force; only the vector sum matters. For an object sliding down a frictionless incline, the net force is the component of gravity along the slope: F_net = mg·sin(theta), giving a = g·sin(theta).

questions.prompt: Property of an object that resists changes in its state of motion, quantified by mass.
questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to maintain its current velocity (whether at rest or moving uniformly) in the absence of a net force. The greater the mass, the greater the inertia — a loaded truck is harder to accelerate or decelerate than an empty one. Newton's first law (the law of inertia, 1687) states that an object's velocity remains constant unless a net force acts on it; this overturned the Aristotelian view that motion requires continuous force. Galileo first articulated the modern concept of inertia in his analysis of frictionless horizontal motion. Mass, measured in kilograms, is the quantitative measure of inertia. On Regents items, students recognize that a passenger continues forward when a car brakes (inertia keeps them moving) and slides outward in a turning car (inertia keeps them moving in a straight line while the car turns).

questions.prompt: Scalar measure of an object's inertia and matter content, measured in kilograms.
questions.explanation: Mass is the scalar measure of how much matter an object contains and how much it resists changes in motion (inertia). SI unit is the kilogram. Mass is intrinsic to the object — it does not change with location, gravity, or motion (at non-relativistic speeds). A 5 kg object has 5 kg of mass on Earth, on the Moon, in deep space, and at the bottom of the ocean. Mass is distinguished from weight, which is the gravitational force on the object (W = mg) and changes with location: a 5 kg object weighs 49 N on Earth but only about 8 N on the Moon. On Regents items, mass appears in F = ma (inertial mass), W = mg (gravitational mass — Einstein's equivalence principle states these are identical), p = mv (momentum), and KE = (1/2)mv^2 (kinetic energy). Mass is conserved in chemical and ordinary physical processes.

questions.prompt: Gravitational force on an object equal to mass times the gravitational field strength, in newtons.
questions.explanation: Weight (W) is the gravitational force exerted by a large body (typically Earth) on the object: W = m·g, where g = 9.81 m/s^2 near Earth's surface (NYS Reference Tables). Weight is a vector directed toward the center of the gravitating body. SI unit is the newton (N). Weight is not the same as mass: a 50 kg student has W = 490 N on Earth but only ~80 N on the Moon, where g_Moon ≈ 1.6 m/s^2. In free fall, an object still has weight (gravity still acts) but feels weightless because no normal force is needed to support it — Einstein's equivalence principle explains this as the indistinguishability of free fall from inertial motion. On Regents items, students compute weight whenever a normal force, tension, or apparent weight problem requires the gravitational force as an input.

questions.prompt: An object's velocity remains constant unless a net external force acts on it.
questions.explanation: Newton's first law (the law of inertia, Principia 1687) states that an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion continues moving in a straight line at constant speed, unless a net external force acts on it. This principle overturned the Aristotelian belief that continuous force is needed to maintain motion. Galileo's frictionless inclined-plane reasoning anticipated this law. The first law identifies inertial reference frames (those in which it holds) and explains everyday phenomena: a passenger continues forward when a car brakes; a ball on a frictionless surface continues rolling indefinitely. On Regents items, the first law applies whenever F_net = 0 — the object is in equilibrium, with either zero velocity or constant velocity. A book at rest on a table has weight balanced by normal force; a parachutist at terminal velocity has gravity balanced by air drag.

questions.prompt: Net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration, F equals m times a.
questions.explanation: Newton's second law (Principia, 1687) quantitatively relates net force, mass, and acceleration: F_net = m·a. The SI unit newton (N) is defined by this equation: 1 N = 1 kg·m/s^2. Acceleration is in the direction of F_net and inversely proportional to mass — a 100 N force accelerates a 10 kg object at 10 m/s^2 but a 100 kg object at only 1 m/s^2. On Regents items, students apply F = ma to find acceleration from net force (or vice versa), often after drawing a free-body diagram. For a frictionless inclined plane at angle theta, F_net = mg·sin(theta), giving a = g·sin(theta) independent of mass. For an Atwood machine (two masses on a pulley), F_net = (m_1 - m_2)g and total inertia is (m_1 + m_2), so a = (m_1 - m_2)g/(m_1 + m_2). The equation applies component-wise.

questions.prompt: For every force, there is an equal-magnitude, opposite-direction force on the other interacting object.
questions.explanation: Newton's third law (Principia, 1687) states that for every force one object exerts on a second, the second exerts a force of equal magnitude in the opposite direction on the first. These action-reaction pairs always act on different objects, never on the same object — so they never cancel in a single free-body diagram. Examples: Earth pulls a falling apple down (gravity), and the apple pulls Earth up with equal force (the apple's effect on Earth is negligible because Earth's mass is enormous); a rocket pushes hot gas out the back, and the gas pushes the rocket forward; a swimmer pushes water backward, and water pushes the swimmer forward. On Regents items, students must identify which forces are third-law pairs (always equal, always different objects) and which are merely equilibrium pairs on the same object (e.g., normal and gravity on a book at rest — these are NOT a third-law pair).

questions.prompt: Two forces that arise from a single interaction, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, acting on different objects.
questions.explanation: An action-reaction pair (Newton's third law, 1687) consists of two forces that arise simultaneously from the same interaction: when object A exerts a force on object B, object B simultaneously exerts an equal-magnitude, opposite-direction force on object A. Key property: the two forces act on different objects — they never appear on the same free-body diagram. Examples: a hammer striking a nail (hammer force on nail = -nail force on hammer); Earth's gravitational pull on the Moon = -Moon's gravitational pull on Earth (Newton's law of universal gravitation). On Regents items, a common error is calling the normal force and weight on a stationary book an action-reaction pair: they are NOT (they act on the same object — the book). The true reaction to weight is the book's gravitational pull on Earth; the true reaction to the normal force is the book's push down on the table.

questions.prompt: State of zero net force, in which an object has zero acceleration and either remains at rest or moves at constant velocity.
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is the condition F_net = 0, meaning the vector sum of all forces on an object is zero. By Newton's second law, this implies a = 0, so the object either remains at rest (static equilibrium) or moves with constant velocity (dynamic equilibrium — Newton's first law). For equilibrium in 2D, both F_x,net = 0 and F_y,net = 0 must hold independently. On Regents items, typical equilibrium problems include: a sign hung from two ropes at different angles (tensions must add vectorially to weight); a block at rest on an incline (friction balances the slope-parallel component of gravity); a ladder against a wall (normal forces and friction balance gravity and torques). A skydiver at terminal velocity is in dynamic equilibrium (gravity equals air resistance). Equilibrium does not mean 'no motion' — it means no acceleration.

questions.prompt: Sketch isolating a single object and showing every force vector acting on it.
questions.explanation: A free-body diagram (FBD) isolates one object and shows every force acting on it as a labeled arrow originating from the object's center, drawn to scale and in the correct direction. Forces from outside (gravity, normal force, friction, tension, applied force, air resistance) are included; internal forces and forces the object exerts on other objects are not. The FBD enables application of Newton's second law: sum forces in x and y, then set each component sum equal to m·a_x or m·a_y. On Regents items, students must include only the forces actually acting (no fictitious forces in inertial frames), label each clearly (W or mg for weight, N for normal, T for tension, f for friction, F_app for applied), and orient them correctly. A common error: drawing centripetal force as a separate arrow — it is the net force on an object in circular motion, not an additional applied force.

questions.prompt: Contact force perpendicular to a surface that pushes outward from the surface, preventing objects from passing through.
questions.explanation: The normal force (N or F_N) is the support force a surface exerts perpendicular to itself on any object in contact with it. It arises from the electromagnetic repulsion between atoms in the surfaces and adjusts in magnitude to whatever is required to prevent the object from penetrating the surface. For a book at rest on a horizontal table: N = mg (vertical equilibrium). On an incline at angle theta: N = mg·cos(theta) (perpendicular to slope). In an accelerating elevator: N exceeds mg when accelerating upward (apparent weight increases) and is less than mg when accelerating downward. In free fall, N = 0 (apparent weightlessness). On Regents items, the normal force is the key input to kinetic friction (f_k = mu_k·N) and changes with applied vertical forces — pulling up on a sliding block reduces N and therefore reduces friction. Normal is always perpendicular to the contact surface.

questions.prompt: Pulling force transmitted along a taut rope, string, cable, or chain.
questions.explanation: Tension (T) is the pulling force transmitted along a rope, string, cable, or chain when stretched taut. Tension is the same throughout an ideal (massless, inextensible) rope and pulls equally on whatever is attached at each end. SI unit is the newton. On Regents items, common tension problems include: hanging weights (T = mg in static equilibrium); Atwood machines (two masses on a pulley — T is the same on both sides for ideal pulley); inclined plane systems; a sign suspended from two ropes at different angles (decompose each tension into x and y, then solve the equilibrium equations). A pulley redirects the tension but does not change its magnitude in the ideal case. If a rope can support up to a certain breaking tension, problems often ask the maximum acceleration or load before failure. Tension is always along the rope's length and pulls inward at each end.

questions.prompt: Force opposing relative motion between two surfaces in contact, parallel to the contact surface.
questions.explanation: Friction is the contact force parallel to a surface that opposes relative sliding (kinetic friction) or impending sliding (static friction). Static friction adjusts up to a maximum value f_s,max = mu_s·N before sliding begins; kinetic friction is f_k = mu_k·N once sliding occurs, with mu_k typically less than mu_s. The NYS Reference Tables lists coefficients of friction for several material combinations (e.g., rubber on dry concrete, steel on steel). Friction always acts opposite to the direction of motion (or impending motion) and is parallel to the contact surface, never perpendicular. On Regents items, students compute f_k from f = mu_k·N, where N may not equal mg if there is a vertical applied force or incline. Friction converts mechanical energy to heat, explaining energy loss in real systems. Air resistance is a type of fluid friction.

questions.prompt: External push or pull on an object, distinct from gravity, normal, tension, or friction.
questions.explanation: An applied force (F_app or F_a) is any external push or pull on an object that is not gravity, normal, tension, friction, or another standard force — typically the force a person, motor, or external agent exerts directly on the object. On a free-body diagram, the applied force is drawn at its actual angle (horizontal, vertical, or inclined). When applied at an angle theta above horizontal, F_app has components F_app·cos(theta) horizontal and F_app·sin(theta) vertical; the vertical component reduces the normal force, which in turn reduces friction (f_k = mu_k·N where N = mg - F_app·sin(theta) for upward-angled pull). On Regents items, applied forces are the most common 'unknown' or 'given' in F_net = m·a problems. Students must always check whether the applied force has components in multiple directions and decompose accordingly. The applied force is the user-controlled term in the force balance.

questions.prompt: Dimensionless ratio of friction force to normal force, with values listed in the NYS Reference Tables.
questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction (mu) is a dimensionless number relating the friction force to the normal force: f = mu·N. Two values exist for each surface pair: the static coefficient mu_s (for surfaces not yet sliding) and the kinetic coefficient mu_k (for surfaces sliding past each other), with mu_s typically larger than mu_k. The NYS Physics Reference Tables provide values for several material pairs (e.g., rubber on dry concrete mu_k ≈ 0.68, waxed ski on snow mu_k ≈ 0.05). mu depends on the materials' microscopic surface properties — roughness, deformability, adhesion — and is approximately independent of contact area and (at moderate speeds) of sliding speed. On Regents items, students look up mu from the reference table, compute N from vertical equilibrium, and find f. A surface that is 'frictionless' is an idealization (mu = 0) used to simplify problems.

questions.prompt: Friction force on a stationary object, adjusting to match applied force up to a maximum value of mu_s times N.
questions.explanation: Static friction (f_s) acts on stationary surfaces and opposes the tendency to slide. Unlike kinetic friction, static friction is variable: it equals exactly the applied force opposing it (so the object remains stationary) until the applied force exceeds the maximum static friction f_s,max = mu_s·N. At that point, the surfaces begin sliding and kinetic friction takes over. A 50 N applied force on a 10 kg block with mu_s = 0.6 and N = 98 N: f_s,max = 58.8 N, so the 50 N force is fully balanced by 50 N of static friction and the block does not move. If the applied force rises to 70 N, it exceeds f_s,max = 58.8 N, the block begins sliding, and kinetic friction (smaller than f_s,max) takes over. On Regents items, students determine whether an object slides by comparing applied force to f_s,max.

questions.prompt: Friction force on a sliding object, equal to mu_k times the normal force and opposing motion.
questions.explanation: Kinetic friction (f_k) acts on surfaces that are already sliding past each other and is given by f_k = mu_k·N, where mu_k is the kinetic coefficient (from the NYS Reference Tables) and N is the normal force. Kinetic friction always opposes the direction of relative sliding motion and is approximately independent of sliding speed (for moderate speeds and non-extreme conditions). Kinetic friction converts kinetic energy into heat — this is why brake pads warm up and why rubbing your hands together makes them warm. On Regents items, students compute f_k from the formula, use it in F_net = ma, and often compute the heat generated as Q = f_k·d (friction force times sliding distance). A sliding block on a horizontal surface decelerates at a = mu_k·g (independent of mass). On an incline, f_k acts up the slope when an object slides downward, and down the slope when it slides upward.

questions.prompt: Drag force opposing motion through a fluid, typically increasing with speed.
questions.explanation: Air resistance (or drag) is a fluid-friction force that opposes an object's motion through air (or any fluid). At low speeds, drag is approximately proportional to speed (F_drag ∝ v); at higher speeds, drag is approximately proportional to speed squared (F_drag ∝ v^2). Drag depends on the object's cross-sectional area, shape (drag coefficient), and air density. On Regents items, air resistance is usually neglected (idealized free fall, idealized projectile motion), but specific items address it: skydivers reach terminal velocity when drag equals weight; a feather falls slowly because its large area-to-mass ratio produces significant drag at low speeds. Air resistance breaks Galileo's heavy-equals-light claim for real falling objects, but in vacuum (or when drag is negligible) all objects fall identically. Without air resistance, a projectile's horizontal velocity remains constant; with it, the projectile decelerates horizontally.

questions.prompt: Constant maximum speed reached by an object falling through a fluid when drag equals weight.
questions.explanation: Terminal velocity (v_t) is the constant speed a falling object reaches when the upward drag force equals the downward gravitational force, producing zero net force and zero acceleration. At v_t: F_drag = mg. A skydiver in a spread-eagle position has a terminal velocity of about 55 m/s (~120 mph); in a streamlined head-down dive, about 90 m/s. Heavier objects have higher terminal velocities (more weight requires more drag, which requires more speed). On Regents items, students recognize that an object at terminal velocity is in equilibrium (Newton's first law), has zero acceleration, and continues at constant velocity. Before reaching v_t, the object has a non-zero downward acceleration (smaller than g due to upward drag). Terminal velocity demonstrates that the no-air-resistance idealization fails for sustained falls. The Mythbusters and other demonstrations have empirically confirmed terminal velocity calculations.

questions.prompt: Surface tilted at an angle theta, on which gravity decomposes into components parallel and perpendicular to the slope.
questions.explanation: An inclined plane at angle theta to the horizontal is a standard Regents geometry. Gravity (mg, downward) decomposes into a component parallel to the slope (mg·sin(theta), directed down the slope) and a component perpendicular to the slope (mg·cos(theta), into the surface, balanced by the normal force). For a frictionless incline, F_net = mg·sin(theta) along the slope, giving acceleration a = g·sin(theta) independent of mass. With kinetic friction (mu_k), the friction force is f_k = mu_k·N = mu_k·mg·cos(theta), opposing motion. If the block slides down: F_net = mg·sin(theta) - mu_k·mg·cos(theta), so a = g(sin theta - mu_k cos theta). On Regents items, students tilt their coordinate system to align x-axis with the slope, simplifying the force decomposition. Galileo used inclined planes to slow down free fall and measure acceleration accurately in the early 1600s.

questions.prompt: Projection of a force vector onto a coordinate axis, found by multiplying the magnitude by sin or cos of the angle.
questions.explanation: A component force is the projection of a force vector along a coordinate axis, found using trigonometry: F_x = F·cos(theta), F_y = F·sin(theta), where theta is the angle from the positive x-axis. Component decomposition reduces 2D vector problems to two independent 1D problems. On an inclined plane, students rotate the axes to align with the slope: gravity has a slope-parallel component mg·sin(theta) and a slope-perpendicular component mg·cos(theta). For a rope pulling at angle phi above horizontal: F_x = F·cos(phi) (horizontal), F_y = F·sin(phi) (vertical, which reduces N if pulling up). On Regents items, the standard procedure is: (1) draw free-body diagram, (2) choose convenient axes, (3) decompose each force into components, (4) sum components separately, (5) apply F_net,x = m·a_x and F_net,y = m·a_y. Component reasoning is foundational to vector mechanics.

questions.prompt: Restoring force exerted by a stretched or compressed spring, equal to the negative of the spring constant times displacement.
questions.explanation: Spring force is the restoring force a spring exerts when stretched or compressed from its natural (relaxed) length: F = -k·x, where k is the spring constant (N/m), x is the displacement from equilibrium, and the negative sign indicates the force is opposite to displacement (Hooke's law, 1660). The NYS Reference Tables list F_s = k·x for magnitude. SI units: F in newtons, x in meters, k in N/m. A stiff spring (large k) requires more force per unit stretch than a flexible one. Spring force is conservative — energy stored is PE_s = (1/2)k·x^2. On Regents items, students compute spring forces, find spring constants from given force-stretch data, and use spring potential energy in conservation problems. The spring force underlies simple harmonic motion (where appropriate), pendulum-like restoring behavior, and oscillation phenomena across physics.

questions.prompt: Force in an ideal spring is directly proportional to its displacement from equilibrium, F equals k times x.
questions.explanation: Hooke's law (Robert Hooke, 1660) states that the force required to stretch or compress an elastic spring is directly proportional to the displacement from its natural length: F = k·x, where k is the spring constant in N/m. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list this relationship for use in spring problems. Hooke's law applies in the linear (elastic) regime; beyond the elastic limit, the spring deforms plastically and no longer obeys the law. On a force vs displacement graph, Hooke's law produces a straight line through the origin with slope k. The area under this line equals the elastic potential energy stored: PE_s = (1/2)k·x^2. On Regents items, students compute k from given (F, x) data points, find the force at a new x, and combine Hooke's law with energy conservation in spring-mass systems. Hooke's law is the simplest example of a restoring force and underlies oscillatory motion analysis.

questions.prompt: Common acceleration of multiple connected objects, found by applying Newton's second law to the entire system.
questions.explanation: When multiple objects are connected (by ropes, contact, pulleys), they move together with the same acceleration — the system acceleration. The standard approach: (1) treat the system as one object with total mass m_total, (2) sum only external forces (internal tensions cancel within the system), (3) apply F_net,external = m_total·a. For an Atwood machine with masses m_1 > m_2 on an ideal pulley: F_net = (m_1 - m_2)g, m_total = m_1 + m_2, so a = (m_1 - m_2)g/(m_1 + m_2). For two blocks pushed together horizontally (block 1 against block 2) with applied force F on block 1: a = F/(m_1 + m_2). The contact force between blocks (an internal force) is then F_contact = m_2·a. On Regents items, students often solve a connected-system problem twice: once for system acceleration, then for internal tensions or contact forces.

questions.prompt: Energy transferred when a force component parallel to displacement acts through a distance, W equals F d cos theta.
questions.explanation: Work (W) is the energy transferred to or from an object by a force acting through a displacement: W = F·d·cos(theta), where F is the force magnitude, d is the displacement magnitude, and theta is the angle between F and d. The SI unit is the joule (J), defined as 1 N·m. Work is a scalar (despite being a dot product of two vectors) and can be positive (force component along motion), negative (force component opposing motion), or zero (force perpendicular to motion, e.g., normal force on a horizontally sliding block, or centripetal force on circular motion). On Regents items, work appears in the work-energy theorem (W_net = ΔKE), in computing energy delivered by an applied force, and in computing energy lost to friction (W_friction = -f_k·d). The NYS Reference Tables formula sheet lists W = F·d, with the cos(theta) factor required when force and displacement are not parallel.

questions.prompt: Rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, P equals W over t, measured in watts.
questions.explanation: Power (P) is the rate of doing work or transferring energy: P = W/t = F·v (for constant force and velocity in the same direction). The SI unit is the watt (W), defined as 1 J/s. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list P = W/t and P = F·v_avg as primary formulas. A 100-watt light bulb consumes 100 J of electrical energy per second. Horsepower (1 hp = 746 W) is an older unit. On Regents items, students compute power for motors lifting loads (P = mgh/t), for cars overcoming friction (P = F·v at constant speed), and for electrical devices (P = IV = I^2·R = V^2/R, see Unit 7). Power is a scalar. Two engines doing the same total work differ in power if they take different times. James Watt's improvements to the steam engine in the late 1700s led to the unit being named in his honor.

questions.prompt: Net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy, W_net equals delta KE.
questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object by all forces equals its change in kinetic energy: W_net = ΔKE = (1/2)m·v_f^2 - (1/2)m·v_i^2. This theorem follows directly from Newton's second law and kinematics (combine F = ma with v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2ad to get F·d = (1/2)m·v_f^2 - (1/2)m·v_i^2). The theorem applies to the NET (total) work — sum of work by all forces (gravity, friction, applied, normal, tension). Forces perpendicular to motion do zero work. On Regents items, the work-energy theorem provides an alternative to kinematics: for a block sliding to rest under friction, W_friction = -f_k·d = 0 - (1/2)mv_i^2, so d = v_i^2/(2·mu_k·g). For an object falling: W_gravity = mgh = (1/2)m·v_f^2, giving v_f = sqrt(2gh) — same as free fall.

questions.prompt: Ratio of useful output work to total input work, expressed as a percentage, always less than 100% in real systems.
questions.explanation: Machine efficiency (eta) is defined as the ratio of useful work output to total work input, usually multiplied by 100 to give a percentage: eta = (W_out/W_in) × 100%. The NYS Reference Tables list efficiency in this form. Real machines always have eta less than 100% because some input energy is lost to friction, heat, sound, deformation, and other dissipative processes (second law of thermodynamics). An ideal frictionless machine would have 100% efficiency, but real pulleys, levers, gears, and engines fall short: a car engine ~30%, an electric motor ~90%, a steam locomotive ~10%. On Regents items, students compute efficiency from (output work)/(input work), recognize that conservative forces alone permit 100% (e.g., a frictionless pendulum, ideal spring), and identify dissipative losses (friction, heat) as the reason real efficiency is below ideal.

questions.prompt: Ratio of output force to input force in a simple machine, often greater than 1 when the machine multiplies force.
questions.explanation: Mechanical advantage (MA) is the ratio of the output force a simple machine produces to the input force the user supplies: MA = F_out/F_in. Simple machines (lever, pulley, wheel and axle, inclined plane, wedge, screw) trade distance for force. A lever with MA = 4 reduces required force to one-quarter the load (but the user must move the input arm four times the distance the load moves). Ideal MA equals the ratio of input distance to output distance (energy conservation: W_in = F_in·d_in = F_out·d_out = W_out, so F_out/F_in = d_in/d_out). Actual MA is less than ideal MA due to friction. On Regents items, students compute MA from force ratios or distance ratios, distinguish ideal from actual MA, and combine MA with efficiency: eta = (actual MA)/(ideal MA). Archimedes (c. 250 BCE) is credited with the foundational analysis of simple machines and the famous quote 'Give me a place to stand, and I will move the Earth' regarding levers.

questions.prompt: Energy of motion equal to one-half mass times velocity squared, KE equals (1/2) m v squared.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy an object possesses due to its motion: KE = (1/2)·m·v^2, where m is mass (kg) and v is speed (m/s). SI unit is the joule (J = kg·m^2/s^2). The NYS Physics Reference Tables list this formula. KE is a scalar, always non-negative, and quadratic in speed — doubling speed quadruples KE. A 1000 kg car at 20 m/s has KE = 200,000 J; at 40 m/s, KE = 800,000 J. The work-energy theorem (W_net = ΔKE) ties KE directly to net force times displacement. On Regents items, students use KE in conservation-of-energy problems (KE at top of hill = KE + PE_loss at bottom), compute speed from energy (v = sqrt(2KE/m)), and recognize that a 'fast-moving' object has more KE than its mass alone suggests. Joule's mid-1800s experiments established the mechanical equivalent of heat, unifying kinetic and thermal energy.

questions.prompt: Energy stored due to position in a gravitational field, PE equals m g h relative to a chosen reference level.
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy (PE_g or U_g) is the energy stored in an object due to its position above a chosen reference level in a gravitational field: PE_g = m·g·h, where h is the height above the reference. SI unit is the joule. The NYS Reference Tables list this formula. PE_g is relative — students must define a zero reference level (ground, table top, lab bench) before assigning values. On Regents items, the standard application is energy conservation: an object falling from height h converts PE_g = m·g·h into KE = (1/2)m·v^2, giving v = sqrt(2gh) at the bottom (same as kinematics). A pendulum swings up and down, periodically converting between PE_g and KE. A roller coaster's height profile determines its energy at each point. The gravitational force F = -dU/dh = -mg points downward (toward decreasing h), consistent with gravity pulling objects down.

questions.prompt: Energy stored in a deformed spring or other elastic medium, PE equals (1/2) k x squared.
questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy (PE_s) is the energy stored in an elastic spring (or other elastic medium) when it is stretched or compressed from its natural length: PE_s = (1/2)·k·x^2, where k is the spring constant (N/m) and x is the displacement from equilibrium (m). The NYS Reference Tables list this formula. The energy is quadratic in displacement: doubling the stretch quadruples the stored energy. Graphically, PE_s equals the area under a Hooke's law force-displacement graph. On Regents items, students use PE_s in conservation problems: a spring compressed by x_0 and released launches a mass with KE = (1/2)k·x_0^2, giving v = sqrt(k·x_0^2/m). For an oscillating spring-mass system, energy continuously converts between PE_s (maximum at extremes) and KE (maximum at equilibrium). Robert Hooke's 1660 spring law underlies this formula.

questions.prompt: Total energy of molecular motion and bonding within an object, often called thermal energy or heat content.
questions.explanation: Internal energy (Q or U_int) is the total kinetic and potential energy of all molecules within an object, including translational, rotational, and vibrational motion plus intermolecular bond energy. It is distinct from the bulk kinetic energy (motion of the whole object) and bulk potential energy (position in external fields). Increasing internal energy typically raises an object's temperature; it can also drive phase changes (melting, vaporization) without temperature change. The SI unit is the joule. On Regents items, internal energy increase from friction is Q = f_k·d (kinetic friction force times sliding distance), and from electrical resistance is Q = I^2·R·t (Joule heating). The first law of thermodynamics relates internal energy change to heat added and work done: ΔU = Q - W. Joule's mid-1800s mechanical-equivalent-of-heat experiments established that mechanical energy can be fully converted to internal energy (heat), unifying mechanics and thermodynamics.

questions.prompt: Sum of kinetic and potential energies, conserved in the absence of nonconservative forces.
questions.explanation: Mechanical energy (E_mech) is the sum of an object's kinetic and potential energies: E_mech = KE + PE = (1/2)mv^2 + mgh + (1/2)kx^2 (whichever PEs are relevant). When only conservative forces (gravity, spring) do work, mechanical energy is conserved: E_mech,i = E_mech,f, allowing direct calculation of speeds, heights, or spring stretches without solving differential equations. Mechanical energy is NOT conserved when nonconservative forces (friction, air resistance, applied forces, normal force doing work) act; instead, ΔE_mech = W_nonconservative. On Regents items, conservation-of-mechanical-energy problems include: a pendulum swinging (KE max at bottom, PE max at top); a ball thrown upward (initial KE converts to PE at peak); a roller coaster (sum of KE + PE constant if frictionless); a spring launching a block (PE_s converts to KE). Mechanical energy is the cornerstone of NY Regents Unit 3.

questions.prompt: Total energy of an isolated system remains constant, with energy transforming between forms but never created or destroyed.
questions.explanation: Conservation of energy is a fundamental law: the total energy of an isolated system is constant — energy transforms between forms (kinetic, potential, thermal, electrical, chemical, nuclear, etc.) but is never created or destroyed. The first law of thermodynamics is the energy-conservation statement for closed systems: ΔU = Q - W. On Regents items, students apply conservation of energy to: a falling object (PE_g → KE, with KE_f = PE_initial if no friction); a pendulum (continuous KE-PE oscillation); a spring-mass system (KE → PE_s → KE); a roller coaster (E_mech constant if frictionless). When friction or other dissipation is present, KE + PE decreases and internal energy (heat) increases by an equal amount — energy is still conserved overall. The NYS Reference Tables emphasize this with the principle 'Total energy is constant.' Joule's mid-1800s heat experiments and Einstein's 1905 E = mc^2 extended conservation to include heat and mass-energy.

questions.prompt: System with no energy or matter exchange across its boundary, in which total energy is conserved.
questions.explanation: A closed system is one across whose boundary no energy or matter flows. In a true closed system, total energy is exactly conserved — no heat, work, or material crosses the boundary, so the first law gives ΔU = 0. Real-world physics problems approximate closed systems to simplify analysis: a pendulum in a vacuum container is approximately closed (negligible air drag, negligible heat loss); a falling object near Earth is closed if we treat 'object + Earth + gravitational field' together. On Regents items, students apply conservation of energy and momentum to closed-system problems: collisions in an isolated tabletop region, oscillations of a vacuum pendulum, motion on a frictionless track. An open system (where energy or matter does cross the boundary) requires tracking the inflows and outflows explicitly. The closed-system assumption is the precondition for conservation laws.

questions.prompt: Force whose work depends on the path taken, removing energy from the mechanical system, such as friction.
questions.explanation: A nonconservative force is one whose work depends on the path taken between two points, not just the endpoints — equivalently, a force that does not have a potential energy function. The most common nonconservative force is friction (kinetic and air drag); others include applied forces, tension when ropes stretch, and viscous fluid drag. Nonconservative forces typically convert mechanical energy (KE + PE) into thermal energy (heat) or other non-mechanical forms, decreasing E_mech. The energy-conservation statement with nonconservative work is ΔE_mech = W_nonconservative (which is negative for friction). Contrast with conservative forces (gravity, ideal spring, electrostatic), whose work depends only on initial and final positions and which DO have potential energy. On Regents items, students compute W_friction = -f_k·d and use it to find energy lost to heat, or to determine how much KE remains after sliding.

questions.prompt: Conversion of useful mechanical energy into less usable thermal energy through friction or other irreversible processes.
questions.explanation: Energy dissipation is the irreversible conversion of organized mechanical energy (KE + PE) into disorganized thermal energy (heat) via friction, air resistance, deformation, or other dissipative processes. Although total energy is conserved, dissipated energy is 'degraded' — it cannot be fully recovered as mechanical work (second law of thermodynamics). A block sliding to a stop on a rough surface dissipates its KE into heat: ΔU_internal = (1/2)m·v_i^2 = mu_k·m·g·d. A pendulum in air slowly loses amplitude as KE/PE converts to heat in the surrounding air. Real machines lose energy to dissipation, reducing their efficiency below 100%. On Regents items, students compute dissipated energy as W_nonconservative (negative), recognize that dissipated heat = decrease in mechanical energy, and use Q = f_k·d for friction heating. Joule's mid-1800s experiments quantified the mechanical equivalent of heat (1 cal = 4.184 J).

questions.prompt: Visual representation showing the magnitudes of KE, PE_g, and PE_s at different points in a process, with total energy conserved if no dissipation.
questions.explanation: An energy bar chart is a visual tool showing the kinetic energy, gravitational potential energy, and elastic potential energy (and any dissipated energy) at different points in a process. Bars at each point should sum to the same total height if no dissipation occurs (conservation of mechanical energy); if dissipation occurs, the total decreases and a 'thermal' or 'lost' bar grows. For a pendulum swinging: at the bottom (PE = 0, KE max); at the top (KE = 0, PE max); at intermediate points (KE + PE = same total). For a block sliding down a rough incline: KE at the bottom is less than PE at the top, with the difference equal to dissipated heat. On Regents items, students fill in bar charts at multiple checkpoints, identify where energy is converting from one form to another, and verify conservation across the chart. Bar charts make the abstract conservation principle visually concrete.

questions.prompt: Stiffness of a spring measured in newtons per meter, equal to the slope of a force versus displacement graph.
questions.explanation: The spring constant (k) measures a spring's stiffness in newtons per meter (N/m). From Hooke's law F = k·x, k is the force required to stretch the spring by 1 meter. Graphically, k is the slope of a force vs displacement (F-x) graph for an ideal spring. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list F_s = k·x and PE_s = (1/2)·k·x^2. A stiff spring (e.g., car suspension, k ~ 50,000 N/m) requires much more force per unit stretch than a flexible one (e.g., slinky, k ~ 1 N/m). The area under the F-x graph equals the elastic potential energy stored. On Regents items, students determine k from given (F, x) data points or from the slope of the F-x graph, then use k in PE_s = (1/2)·k·x^2 for energy conservation problems with springs (e.g., a block compressed against a spring, released, and launched).

questions.prompt: Graph of potential energy versus position, whose negative slope at any point gives the conservative force on the object.
questions.explanation: A potential energy curve is a graph of an object's potential energy (PE) versus position (x). The negative slope at any point gives the conservative force: F = -dU/dx. Minima in the curve represent stable equilibrium points (force zero, restoring); maxima represent unstable equilibrium (force zero, destabilizing). For a simple spring: PE_s = (1/2)k·x^2 is an upward parabola with minimum at x = 0 — the equilibrium position. For gravity near Earth: PE_g = mgh is a straight line with slope mg — gravity pulls down (F = -dU/dh = -mg). On Regents items, students read PE curves to identify equilibrium positions, find turning points (where total energy equals PE, so KE = 0), and determine the direction of force from slope. The shape of a PE curve encodes the dynamics: 'walking down a slope' on the curve gains KE; 'walking up' loses KE. Bound motion occurs in PE wells between turning points.

questions.prompt: Position where an object's velocity momentarily reaches zero, where total energy equals potential energy.
questions.explanation: A turning point is a position where an object's KE = 0 (velocity is momentarily zero) and the total mechanical energy equals the potential energy there: E_total = PE(x_turning). At a turning point, the object reverses direction. For a pendulum: turning points are the highest points of the swing (KE = 0, PE max). For a spring-mass oscillator with amplitude A: turning points are at x = ±A (where all energy is elastic PE). For a ball thrown vertically: the peak is the turning point (KE = 0, PE max). For a particle moving in a potential well: turning points are where the horizontal total-energy line intersects the PE curve. On Regents items, students find turning points by setting KE = 0 and solving PE(x) = E_total, then identify where the object reverses direction. The turning point concept generalizes to any bound oscillation in any PE curve.

questions.prompt: Chosen position at which gravitational potential energy is defined to be zero, a convention that simplifies calculations.
questions.explanation: A zero reference level is an arbitrarily chosen position (height) at which gravitational potential energy is defined to be zero (PE_g = 0). The choice is a matter of convenience — common choices include ground level, the bottom of the trajectory, or the launch point. Only differences in PE_g matter physically, so the reference level affects only the numerical values of PE and total energy, never the result of any physics calculation. For a roller coaster, taking the lowest point as the reference simplifies the equation E_mech = (1/2)mv^2 + mgh. For a pendulum, the lowest point is the natural reference. On Regents items, students must state their reference level explicitly when assigning PE values, then apply conservation consistently. Choosing a 'good' reference reduces algebraic complexity (e.g., the reference at the start ensures initial PE = 0).

questions.prompt: Inclined-plane scenario in which an object's gravitational PE converts to KE or work against friction.
questions.explanation: A ramp problem is the inclined-plane application of energy conservation: an object on a ramp at height h converts gravitational PE = m·g·h to kinetic energy at the bottom. Without friction: m·g·h = (1/2)m·v^2, so v = sqrt(2gh) (independent of ramp angle and mass). With kinetic friction: m·g·h = (1/2)m·v^2 + mu_k·m·g·cos(theta)·d, where d = h/sin(theta) is the ramp length. Solving: v = sqrt(2gh - 2·mu_k·g·cos(theta)·d) = sqrt(2gh(1 - mu_k/tan(theta))). For the object to slide at all, mu_s < tan(theta); for it to accelerate down, mu_k < tan(theta). On Regents items, ramp problems combine energy conservation, friction calculation, and kinematic outputs (final speed, sliding distance, time on the ramp). Galileo's inclined-plane experiments in the early 1600s used ramps to slow down free fall and measure acceleration accurately.

questions.prompt: Oscillating motion of a mass on a string, with continuous conversion between KE and gravitational PE.
questions.explanation: A pendulum swing is the canonical example of mechanical energy conservation. A mass m on a string of length L is raised to height h above the lowest point and released. At the highest points (turning points): KE = 0, PE = m·g·h, so E_total = m·g·h. At the lowest point: PE = 0 (taking the bottom as reference), KE = E_total, so (1/2)m·v_max^2 = m·g·h, giving v_max = sqrt(2gh). At intermediate angles theta from vertical: h = L(1 - cos(theta)), and energy conservation gives v(theta) = sqrt(2gL(1 - cos(theta))). For small amplitudes, the period is T = 2·pi·sqrt(L/g) — approximately independent of amplitude (Galileo's discovery, 1581, while observing a chandelier in the Pisa cathedral). On Regents items, students apply energy conservation to find speed at any height in the swing, identify turning points and the equilibrium position, and compute period from the formula.

questions.prompt: Idealized track scenario in which mechanical energy is conserved across hills, loops, and dips of varying height.
questions.explanation: A roller coaster model applies conservation of mechanical energy across a track of varying height: at any point, (1/2)mv^2 + mgh = E_total = constant (if frictionless). The highest point's energy must exceed any subsequent point's energy, so the first hill is always the tallest in an unpowered coaster. At the top of a loop of radius r: minimum speed v_min satisfies the centripetal force condition (Unit 5): v_min^2 = g·r (otherwise the car loses contact with the track). The minimum drop height to clear a loop of radius r is h ≥ 2.5·r (from energy conservation: m·g·h = (1/2)m·v_min^2 + m·g·2r, with v_min^2 = g·r). On Regents items, students compute speed at any point from the height difference, identify the minimum starting height to clear a loop, and account for friction (lost energy = work by friction along the track). Real coasters lose ~10-30% of initial energy to friction across a full ride.

questions.prompt: Distance required to stop a moving object, equal to KE divided by the magnitude of the braking force.
questions.explanation: Braking distance is the distance required for a moving object (typically a car) to come to rest under a decelerating force. By the work-energy theorem: W_brake = -F_brake·d = 0 - (1/2)m·v^2, so d = m·v^2/(2·F_brake). For a car decelerating only via kinetic friction (locked-wheel skid): F_brake = mu_k·m·g, giving d = v^2/(2·mu_k·g) — independent of mass! Doubling speed quadruples braking distance (quadratic in v). On Regents items, this explains why higher speed limits dramatically increase stopping distances and why anti-lock brakes (using rolling friction, mu_s > mu_k) can stop a car faster than locked-wheel skidding. Real braking distance depends on tire-road friction coefficient (mu_k on dry concrete ≈ 0.7; on wet concrete ≈ 0.4; on ice ≈ 0.1), reaction time before brakes engage, and the antilock-brake system's effectiveness.

questions.prompt: Computation of eta equals (W_output / W_input) × 100%, with values below 100% indicating energy dissipated.
questions.explanation: An efficiency calculation determines what fraction of input energy is converted to useful output: eta = (W_out/W_in) × 100% (NYS Reference Tables). A pulley system that lifts a 100 N load by 1 m with 150 J of input energy has efficiency eta = (100 J)/(150 J) × 100% = 66.7%, with 50 J lost to friction and heat. Real-world efficiencies vary: incandescent light bulb ~5% (most energy is heat); LED ~30-50%; car engine ~25-30%; electric motor ~85-95%; steam turbine ~40%; hydroelectric ~90%. On Regents items, students compute efficiency from given input and useful output (work, energy, force-distance products), interpret efficiency in the context of the second law (some energy always dissipates as heat), and explain why higher efficiency reduces energy waste and operational cost. Engineering progress is often measured in efficiency improvements (Watt's steam engine improvements ~10× efficiency over Newcomen).

questions.prompt: Vector product of mass and velocity, p equals m v, measured in kilogram meters per second.
questions.explanation: Momentum (p) is a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity: p = m·v. SI unit is kg·m/s (equivalent to N·s). The NYS Physics Reference Tables list this formula. Momentum carries direction — a 1000 kg car moving 20 m/s east has momentum 20,000 kg·m/s east. Newton's second law in its original form was stated by Newton in the 1687 Principia as F = dp/dt (force equals rate of change of momentum), reducing to F = ma for constant mass. The total momentum of a system is the vector sum of individual momenta. On Regents items, students compute momentum, recognize that doubling either mass or speed doubles momentum, and use momentum in collision and impulse problems. Momentum is conserved in any system with zero external net force (isolated system) — a foundational principle of physics from Newton through quantum mechanics and Einstein's relativity.

questions.prompt: Vector product of force and time, J equals F times delta t, equal to change in momentum.
questions.explanation: Impulse (J or simply F·Δt) is the vector product of force and the time interval during which it acts: J = F·Δt. SI unit is N·s = kg·m/s — the same as momentum. The impulse-momentum theorem states that impulse equals the change in momentum: J = Δp = m·v_f - m·v_i. The NYS Reference Tables list this relationship. Graphically, impulse is the area under a force-time graph. On Regents items, impulse appears in collision problems (a baseball bat applying force F for time Δt changes the ball's momentum), in stopping problems (brakes applying friction over time stop a car), and in safety applications (airbags increase Δt to reduce required F). A short, hard hit and a long, soft push can deliver the same impulse and produce the same momentum change.

questions.prompt: Net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum, F times delta t equals delta p.
questions.explanation: The impulse-momentum theorem states that the net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: F_net·Δt = Δp = m·v_f - m·v_i. This is Newton's second law in integral form (F = dp/dt integrated over time). The NYS Reference Tables list J = F·Δt = Δp. On Regents items, the theorem provides a powerful alternative to F = ma when forces vary or act briefly. For an egg dropped on the floor (Δt very small), F is large; on a pillow (Δt larger), F is smaller — the change in momentum (m·v) is the same, but the force depends on the stopping time. For a force-time graph, impulse equals the area under the curve, and equals Δp. The theorem underlies safety engineering: longer collision times reduce peak forces and injury risk.

questions.prompt: Final momentum minus initial momentum, delta p equals m v_f minus m v_i, equal to net impulse.
questions.explanation: Change in momentum (Δp) is a vector quantity equal to the final momentum minus the initial momentum: Δp = m·v_f - m·v_i (assuming constant mass). For a 1 kg ball thrown at 10 m/s right that bounces off a wall and returns at 8 m/s left: Δp = (1)(-8) - (1)(10) = -18 kg·m/s — a substantial change due to the direction reversal. Note that even when |v| barely changes, a direction reversal produces a large Δp. The impulse-momentum theorem states that this change equals the net impulse on the object: Δp = F_net·Δt. On Regents items, students compute Δp with careful attention to vector signs (Δp can be much larger than mv_f alone if the direction reverses), then relate Δp to impulse to find force or time. This is the heart of collision analysis.

questions.prompt: Vector sum of momenta of all objects in a system, conserved when net external force is zero.
questions.explanation: System momentum is the vector sum of momenta of all objects in a chosen system: p_system = sum of m_i·v_i. The law of conservation of momentum states that the system's total momentum is constant if no external force acts on the system (isolated system). Internal forces (the objects interacting with each other via collision, explosion, spring, etc.) cannot change total momentum because they come in equal-opposite pairs (Newton's third law) — they cancel within the system. On Regents items, conservation of momentum is the key tool for collision problems: m_1·v_1i + m_2·v_2i = m_1·v_1f + m_2·v_2f (assuming straight-line motion). For 2D, both components are separately conserved. A cannon firing a cannonball: initial system momentum = 0, so afterward (cannon momentum) + (cannonball momentum) = 0, giving the cannon recoil velocity. Conservation of momentum is one of the most powerful laws in physics.

questions.prompt: Collision in which both kinetic energy and momentum are conserved.
questions.explanation: An elastic collision is a collision in which both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved. Examples: ideal billiard balls, atomic-scale collisions, two carts on an air track with bumpers, hard balls bouncing off rigid walls. The conservation equations are: m_1·v_1i + m_2·v_2i = m_1·v_1f + m_2·v_2f (momentum) AND (1/2)m_1·v_1i^2 + (1/2)m_2·v_2i^2 = (1/2)m_1·v_1f^2 + (1/2)m_2·v_2f^2 (KE). These two equations can be solved simultaneously for the two unknown final velocities given the initial conditions and masses. A special case: equal-mass elastic collision (1D, one initially at rest) transfers all velocity from the moving ball to the stationary one. On Regents items, the test usually identifies whether a collision is elastic and asks students to apply the relevant conservation laws. Truly elastic collisions are rare in macroscopic physics (most collisions lose some energy to heat, sound, deformation).

questions.prompt: Collision in which momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not, with some KE converted to heat or deformation.
questions.explanation: An inelastic collision is one in which momentum IS conserved but kinetic energy is NOT — some KE is converted to internal energy (heat, deformation, sound). Most real-world collisions are inelastic to some degree. Cars colliding, balls of clay striking, sports collisions, falling objects sticking to surfaces — all inelastic. The momentum conservation equation still applies: m_1·v_1i + m_2·v_2i = m_1·v_1f + m_2·v_2f. The change in KE between initial and final equals the energy dissipated (negative, since final KE < initial KE).
