On Regents items, students apply momentum conservation to find final velocities, then compare initial and final KE to find energy lost. A common Regents scenario: a moving cart collides with and rebounds from a stationary cart; the difference between initial and final total KE is the energy lost to friction, deformation, and heat.

questions.prompt: Inelastic collision in which the colliding objects stick together and move with a single common velocity afterward.
questions.explanation: A perfectly (or completely) inelastic collision is the extreme case of an inelastic collision: the objects stick together after impact and share a single common final velocity. The momentum equation simplifies: m_1·v_1i + m_2·v_2i = (m_1 + m_2)·v_f, where v_f is the common final velocity. This is the most KE-dissipative type of collision (maximum energy goes to heat/deformation). Examples: a ball of clay sticking to a wall; a dart embedding in a target; train cars coupling together. On Regents items, a typical scenario: a 0.5 kg dart at 20 m/s embeds in a 1.5 kg block at rest. By momentum conservation: (0.5)(20) + 0 = (0.5 + 1.5)·v_f, so v_f = 10/2 = 5 m/s. Initial KE = (1/2)(0.5)(20^2) = 100 J; final KE = (1/2)(2)(5^2) = 25 J. Energy lost = 75 J (to heat, deformation, sound). The ballistic pendulum is the classic perfectly inelastic example.

questions.prompt: Backward motion of one object as another is ejected forward, with total momentum conserved at zero.
questions.explanation: Recoil is the backward motion of an object after it ejects another object forward — a direct consequence of conservation of momentum. If the initial system momentum is zero (system at rest), the final momenta must sum to zero: m_1·v_1 + m_2·v_2 = 0, so v_1 = -(m_2/m_1)·v_2 (heavier object recoils slower). Classic examples: a cannon firing a cannonball (cannon kicks back); a rifle's recoil into the shooter's shoulder; a rocket ejecting hot exhaust (rocket accelerates forward); ice skaters pushing off each other; a person stepping off a stationary boat (boat moves backward). On Regents items, students compute recoil velocity from m_1·v_1 = -m_2·v_2, recognizing that the heavier object recoils with smaller speed but the same magnitude of momentum. Rocket propulsion is a continuous recoil — Newton's third law (1687) is the foundational principle.

questions.prompt: Inverse collision in which an object at rest separates into pieces flying outward with conservation of momentum.
questions.explanation: An explosion is the inverse of a perfectly inelastic collision: an initially stationary object (or one moving with some velocity) breaks apart into multiple pieces, with the total momentum conserved. If the object is initially at rest, the final momenta of all fragments must vector-sum to zero. For a 2-piece explosion at rest: m_1·v_1 + m_2·v_2 = 0, so v_1 and v_2 are opposite (with magnitudes inversely proportional to mass). Energy is NOT conserved (KE increases — the source is chemical, nuclear, or mechanical PE stored in the original object, which converts to KE of the fragments). On Regents items, explosion problems include: a stationary firework breaking into pieces; a person on ice pushing off another person (each gains momentum, total still zero); a compressed spring releasing two masses. Conservation of momentum is the only relevant conservation law; KE is created from the stored PE.

questions.prompt: System with no external forces, in which total momentum is conserved.
questions.explanation: An isolated system is one experiencing no external forces (or whose net external force is zero) — the precondition for conservation of momentum. Internal forces (interactions among objects within the system) come in Newton's third law pairs and cancel within the system, so they cannot change total momentum. External forces (gravity from outside the system, applied forces from outside, friction from a non-system surface) CAN change total momentum. A pair of carts on a frictionless track form an isolated system in the horizontal direction (gravity is vertical and balanced by normal force). Two ice skaters on frictionless ice are isolated horizontally. A car on a road is NOT isolated horizontally (friction from the road is external). On Regents items, students must check the isolation condition before applying momentum conservation: 'Is there an external horizontal force? If no, momentum in that direction is conserved.'

questions.prompt: Force between objects within a chosen system, which cannot change the system's total momentum.
questions.explanation: An internal force is any force between two objects that are both inside the chosen system. Internal forces come in Newton's third-law pairs (force of A on B = -force of B on A), so they always sum to zero within the system. As a result, internal forces cannot change the system's total momentum — only external forces can. When two carts collide on a track, the forces they exert on each other are internal to the cart-cart system; the total momentum is conserved regardless of how complicated the internal collision physics is. Internal forces CAN change individual objects' momenta (and rearrange them within the system), but the total stays the same. On Regents items, students recognize that collisional forces, spring forces between objects in the system, and gravity between system objects are internal and do not appear in the momentum conservation equation.

questions.prompt: Momentum's vector nature, with separate conservation of x- and y-components in two-dimensional problems.
questions.explanation: Vector momentum reflects that p = m·v is a vector quantity, with separate components in each direction. In 2D problems (e.g., glancing collisions, projectiles), momentum is conserved component-by-component: p_x,i,total = p_x,f,total AND p_y,i,total = p_y,f,total, simultaneously and independently. This produces two equations for each collision, which (combined with KE conservation for elastic collisions) allow solving for all unknown components. Common Regents 2D scenarios: two carts colliding at an angle (decompose initial velocities into x and y; conservation in each); a stationary firework exploding into 3 pieces (vector sum of final momenta = 0). The vector nature of momentum means even a glancing collision in which speeds don't change has a Δp (because direction does). Tracking momentum as a vector — with signs, components, or directional notation — is essential to correct Regents responses.

questions.prompt: Weighted average position of mass in a system, which moves with constant velocity in the absence of external forces.
questions.explanation: The center of mass (CM) is the weighted-average position of mass in a system: x_CM = (sum of m_i·x_i)/(sum of m_i), similarly for y_CM. The CM of a uniform symmetric object lies at the geometric center; for a two-particle system, the CM lies on the line connecting them, closer to the heavier one. Newton's second law applies to the CM: F_net,external = M_total·a_CM. If F_net,external = 0, the CM moves with constant velocity (or remains at rest). This means in an explosion, even though the fragments fly apart, their CM continues moving as if the explosion never occurred. In a collision, the CM velocity before equals the CM velocity after. On Regents items, the CM concept usually appears qualitatively or via simple weighted-average problems. The CM is the 'representative point' of any extended system for Newton's laws.

questions.prompt: Plot of applied force versus time, whose area equals impulse delivered to the object.
questions.explanation: A force-time (F-t) graph plots the applied force on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis. The area under the F-t curve between two times equals the impulse delivered: J = integral of F dt = area. For a constant force F applied for time Δt, the area is a rectangle: J = F·Δt. For a triangular F-t pulse (force rises linearly to a peak then falls back to zero, total time Δt, peak force F_peak): J = (1/2)·F_peak·Δt. The impulse-momentum theorem connects this to Δp = J. On Regents items, students compute impulse as the area under a gridded F-t graph (counting squares or using rectangle/triangle/trapezoid formulas), then equate to Δp to find the change in velocity. F-t graphs are the visual analog of v-t graphs (where area is displacement) — both encode an accumulated quantity via integration.

questions.prompt: Region under a force-time graph, numerically equal to the impulse delivered in newton-seconds.
questions.explanation: Area under a force-time graph is impulse — the geometric interpretation of the impulse-momentum theorem. The units check: N · s = (kg·m/s^2)·s = kg·m/s, which is the unit of momentum. For a constant force: impulse = F · Δt = rectangle area = (height)(width). For a varying force: impulse = the irregular area under the curve, computed by adding rectangles, triangles, trapezoids, or counting grid squares. For two F-t graphs delivering the same impulse: a tall narrow rectangle and a short wide rectangle give the same Δp. On Regents items, this is the most common 'read-a-graph' impulse problem: students count area under a piecewise-linear F-t curve and equate to Δp = m(v_f - v_i) to find the final velocity. The technique is identical to finding displacement from a v-t graph, just with different physical quantities.

questions.prompt: Duration of contact during a collision, which determines the average force given a fixed momentum change.
questions.explanation: Collision time (Δt) is the duration of contact during a collision. From the impulse-momentum theorem F_avg·Δt = Δp, for a fixed momentum change Δp, the average force F_avg is inversely proportional to Δt: F_avg = Δp/Δt. A baseball bat in contact with a ball for 0.001 s applies a much larger force than a foam paddle in contact for 0.1 s for the same momentum change. Collision times for hard-hard contact (steel-on-steel) are typically very short (milliseconds), producing large peak forces. Soft contacts (rubber, foam, padding) extend Δt, dramatically reducing peak forces — the principle behind airbags, helmets, and crumple zones. On Regents items, students use F = Δp/Δt to compute average force, recognize that doubling Δt halves F (for the same Δp), and explain the safety benefit of cushioning materials.

questions.prompt: Time-averaged force during a collision, equal to change in momentum divided by collision time.
questions.explanation: Average force during a collision is F_avg = Δp/Δt — the impulse-momentum theorem rearranged. For an 0.15 kg baseball changing velocity from 40 m/s (toward pitcher) to 50 m/s (away from pitcher) during 0.001 s contact with a bat: Δp = (0.15)(50 - (-40)) = 13.5 kg·m/s (note the sign reversal!), so F_avg = 13.5/0.001 = 13,500 N. This is the average; peak force during the collision is even higher. On Regents items, students compute F_avg from Δp and Δt, paying careful attention to direction signs when a velocity reverses. F_avg is the height of the equivalent rectangle on an F-t graph that has the same area as the actual collision curve. F_avg, multiplied by Δt, gives the same impulse and hence the same Δp as the actual time-varying force.

questions.prompt: Maximum instantaneous force during a collision, generally larger than the time-averaged force.
questions.explanation: Peak force is the maximum instantaneous force achieved during a collision, occurring at the moment of maximum deformation/compression. For a sinusoidal or roughly triangular force-time pulse, peak force is typically about 2× the average force (F_peak ≈ 2·F_avg for a half-sine pulse; F_peak = 2·F_avg for a triangular pulse). For a hard, brief collision (steel ball on steel anvil), peak force can be enormous despite small impulse. Safety engineering aims to reduce peak force (which causes injury or material failure) by extending collision time, even though Δp and impulse remain the same. On Regents items, students may be asked to identify peak force as the highest point on an F-t graph and to compare it to the average force (rectangle of same area). A car's crumple zone reduces peak force on the passenger compartment by spreading the collision over a longer time and larger deformation distance.

questions.prompt: Inflatable cushion that extends collision time during a crash to reduce peak force on the occupant.
questions.explanation: An airbag is an inflatable cushion in a vehicle that deploys rapidly during a crash to reduce the peak force on the occupant by extending the collision time. The physics: the occupant has momentum p = m·v immediately before impact. To bring p to zero, the same impulse F·Δt = Δp must be delivered, regardless of mechanism. Without an airbag, the head/chest hits the steering wheel or dashboard with a very short Δt (milliseconds) and very large F. With an airbag, Δt is extended to ~100 ms and F decreases proportionally — typically by 5-10×. Combined with seatbelts (which spread force over the chest and pelvis), airbags dramatically reduce injury severity. On Regents items, this is the canonical real-world impulse-momentum theorem application: 'Explain why an airbag reduces injury' (answer: it extends collision time, reducing peak force for the same change in momentum).

questions.prompt: Region of a vehicle designed to deform during impact, extending collision time and absorbing energy.
questions.explanation: A crumple zone is a deformable structural region at the front and rear of a vehicle designed to crush in a controlled way during a collision. The physics rationale: by extending the distance and time over which the vehicle decelerates, the average force on the passenger compartment is reduced (F = Δp/Δt). Modern car bodies have a rigid 'safety cell' surrounding passengers and deliberately weak (or progressively crushable) crumple zones outside it. In a head-on collision, the crumple zones absorb energy via plastic deformation (converting KE to internal energy of bent steel), reducing both peak force and total energy transferred to occupants. On Regents items, crumple zones illustrate the impulse-momentum theorem (extend Δt to reduce F) and conservation of energy (KE dissipated as deformation work). Combined with airbags and seatbelts, crumple zones make modern vehicles dramatically safer than mid-20th-century cars.

questions.prompt: Foam cushioning in a helmet that extends impact time to reduce peak force on the head during a collision.
questions.explanation: Helmet padding is a foam or gel cushion that extends the collision time when the head strikes an object, reducing the peak force transmitted to the skull and brain. For a head moving at 5 m/s that comes to rest in 0.001 s (no padding) vs 0.01 s (with foam): F_avg drops by 10×. The padding crushes during impact, absorbing energy (KE → internal energy of foam deformation) and lengthening Δt. Helmet standards (Snell, DOT, ECE) specify maximum allowable peak forces for various impact scenarios. On Regents items, helmet padding illustrates the impulse-momentum theorem applied to head injury prevention: 'Why does a helmet help?' — extending Δt reduces F. Concussion physics is more nuanced (rotational acceleration matters too), but the impulse-momentum argument captures the core protective mechanism for linear impacts.

questions.prompt: Technique of extending hand or bat contact time with a ball to maximize impulse and resulting momentum change.
questions.explanation: Follow-through in baseball, tennis, golf, and similar sports is the technique of continuing the swing after contact rather than stopping abruptly. This extends the time of contact (Δt) between the implement (bat, racquet, club) and the ball, delivering greater impulse F·Δt = Δp and therefore producing a larger change in momentum (faster outgoing ball). The physics: even a small extension of contact time, with reasonable force, adds significantly to the impulse. A coach's instruction to 'follow through' is, in physics terms, an instruction to maximize Δt. On Regents items, follow-through illustrates the impulse-momentum theorem from the offensive side: while airbags and crumple zones extend Δt to reduce force, follow-through extends Δt to increase impulse (and hence Δp) for the same applied force. The opposite tradeoff but the same physics principle.

questions.prompt: Time required to bring a moving object to rest, equal to mv divided by the average decelerating force.
questions.explanation: Stopping time is the time required to bring a moving object to rest: from F_avg·Δt = Δp = m·v (assuming final v = 0), we get Δt = m·v/F_avg. A car braking from 30 m/s under a friction force of 7000 N (m = 1000 kg): Δt = (1000)(30)/7000 ≈ 4.3 s. A skydiver opening a parachute uses drag force to extend stopping time and reduce peak force. On Regents items, stopping time problems combine momentum and impulse with safety design: increasing stopping time reduces peak force on occupants (for the same Δp). Anti-lock brakes reduce stopping distance but may not reduce stopping time as much as one might expect; the safety advantage is maintaining steering control. Stopping time is closely related to stopping distance (Δd = average velocity × Δt = (v_i/2)·Δt for uniform deceleration).

questions.prompt: Center-directed acceleration of an object moving in a circle, a_c equals v squared over r.
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration (a_c) is the acceleration of an object moving along a circular path, directed toward the center of the circle. Its magnitude is a_c = v^2/r, where v is the speed and r is the radius of the circle. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list this formula. Even at constant speed, circular motion involves acceleration because the velocity vector continuously changes direction (recall velocity is a vector). For a car going 20 m/s around a curve of 50 m radius: a_c = (20)^2/50 = 8 m/s^2. Centripetal means 'center-seeking' — the acceleration points from the object toward the center of the circle. On Regents items, students compute a_c from v and r, recognize that doubling speed quadruples a_c (quadratic), and apply Newton's second law: the net force on a circling object equals m·a_c and is directed toward the center.

questions.prompt: Time for one complete revolution around a circle, T, related to frequency by T equals 1 over f.
questions.explanation: Period (T) is the time for one complete cycle or revolution. SI unit is the second. For circular motion, T is the time for one full trip around the circle: T = 2·pi·r/v, where r is the radius and v is the speed. The NYS Reference Tables list T = 1/f, where f is the frequency in hertz (cycles per second). A bicycle wheel rotating at 5 rev/s has T = 0.2 s. A satellite orbiting Earth at low altitude has T ≈ 90 minutes; the Moon orbits at T ≈ 27 days. On Regents items, period appears in: angular velocity calculations (omega = 2·pi/T); centripetal acceleration with the substitution v = 2·pi·r/T, giving a_c = 4·pi^2·r/T^2; satellite motion via Kepler's third law (T^2 proportional to r^3 for orbits around the same central body).

questions.prompt: Number of complete revolutions per second, f, measured in hertz and equal to 1 over T.
questions.explanation: Frequency (f) is the number of complete cycles per unit time. SI unit is the hertz (Hz), defined as 1 cycle per second (s^-1). The NYS Reference Tables list f = 1/T. A 60-Hz generator completes 60 full rotations per second; a typical AM radio carrier might be 1 MHz = 10^6 Hz. For circular motion: omega = 2·pi·f (angular frequency in rad/s). For waves: v = f·lambda (wave speed equals frequency times wavelength, Unit 9). On Regents items, frequency appears in circular motion (revolutions per second), AC electricity (60 Hz in the US, 50 Hz in Europe), waves (sound, light, radio), and quantum physics (E = h·f, Planck's relation, Unit 10). High frequency means short period and rapid oscillation. Heinrich Hertz (1887) first generated and detected radio waves, confirming Maxwell's electromagnetic theory and earning him the unit now bearing his name.

questions.prompt: Linear speed of an object moving along a circular path, equal to the radius times angular velocity.
questions.explanation: Tangential speed (v) is the linear speed of an object moving along a circular path — the speed an observer outside the circle would measure. It is related to angular velocity by v = r·omega, where r is the radius and omega is the angular velocity in rad/s. For uniform circular motion: v = 2·pi·r/T = 2·pi·r·f. The tangential velocity vector is directed tangent to the circle at each instant — perpendicular to the radius. On Regents items, tangential speed appears in centripetal acceleration calculations (a_c = v^2/r), in projectile-like problems (e.g., a ball released from a horizontally swinging string becomes a projectile with horizontal velocity equal to the tangential speed at release), and in rotational mechanics. Two points on a rotating rigid body have the same angular velocity but different tangential speeds (proportional to their distances from the axis): the rim of a wheel moves faster than its hub.

questions.prompt: Attractive force between two masses, F_g equals G m_1 m_2 over r squared (Newton's law of universal gravitation).
questions.explanation: Gravitational force is the attractive force between any two masses, given by Newton's universal law of gravitation (Principia, 1687): F_g = G·m_1·m_2/r^2, where G = 6.67 × 10^-11 N·m^2/kg^2 is the universal gravitational constant (NYS Reference Tables), m_1 and m_2 are the masses, and r is the distance between their centers. The force is attractive and acts along the line connecting them. Two masses each of 1 kg, 1 m apart, experience a force of only 6.67 × 10^-11 N — gravity is the weakest fundamental force at human scales. Near Earth's surface, F_g = m·g where g = G·M_Earth/R_Earth^2 ≈ 9.81 m/s^2. On Regents items, students compute gravitational force between planets, between satellites and Earth, and between objects with different separations. Newton's law unified terrestrial gravity (apple falling) with celestial gravity (Moon orbiting), one of the great unifications in physics.

questions.prompt: Region of space where a mass experiences a gravitational force per unit mass, g equals F_g over m.
questions.explanation: A gravitational field is a region of space in which a mass experiences a gravitational force. The field strength (g) is defined as the force per unit mass: g = F_g/m, with SI units of N/kg (equivalent to m/s^2). The NYS Reference Tables provide g = G·M_central/r^2 as the field strength a distance r from a central mass M. Near Earth's surface, g = 9.81 N/kg downward. The field decreases with the inverse square of distance: at twice Earth's radius from the center, g is one-fourth its surface value. The field is a vector pointing toward the central mass. On Regents items, students compute g at different distances (e.g., at the Moon's distance from Earth, g = 0.0027 m/s^2), interpret weight as W = m·g, and use g in PE_g = m·g·h (valid near the surface where g is approximately constant). Field thinking, developed by Faraday (1831) and Maxwell (1864), generalizes to electricity and magnetism.

questions.prompt: Pattern in which a quantity decreases as 1 over r squared with distance, applying to gravity, electric force, and light intensity.
questions.explanation: An inverse-square law states that a physical quantity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source: quantity ∝ 1/r^2. Newton's law of gravitation (F_g = G·m_1·m_2/r^2, Principia 1687) and Coulomb's law of electrostatic force (F = k·q_1·q_2/r^2, 1785) are the two foundational inverse-square laws in physics. Light intensity from a point source also follows 1/r^2 (the same total energy spreads over a sphere whose area is 4·pi·r^2, doubling in area each time r doubles). The geometric origin: any influence spreading uniformly in 3D space dilutes its intensity by the area of an expanding sphere, which goes as r^2. On Regents items, students recognize that doubling the distance reduces force/intensity by a factor of 4, and tripling reduces by a factor of 9. Modern physics extends this pattern: light from stars, sound from explosions, and radiation from a point source all dilute as 1/r^2.

questions.prompt: Speed at which a satellite circles a planet, v equals square root of G M over r for a circular orbit.
questions.explanation: Orbital speed (v_orbit) is the speed at which a satellite must travel to maintain a circular orbit at radius r around a central mass M. From Newton's second law applied to circular motion: gravitational force = centripetal force, so G·M·m/r^2 = m·v^2/r, giving v_orbit = sqrt(G·M/r). For a satellite in low Earth orbit (r ≈ R_Earth ≈ 6.37 × 10^6 m): v ≈ 7.9 km/s (about 17,500 mph), period T ≈ 90 minutes. The Moon orbits Earth at v ≈ 1 km/s, r ≈ 384,000 km, T ≈ 27.3 days. Note: orbital speed depends on r but not on the satellite's mass (just as free-fall acceleration is independent of mass). On Regents items, students compute orbital speed from sqrt(G·M/r), find period via T = 2·pi·r/v_orbit, and connect to Kepler's third law T^2 = (4·pi^2/GM)·r^3.

questions.prompt: Orbital motion of an object around a central body, with gravity supplying the centripetal force.
questions.explanation: Satellite motion is the orbital motion of an object around a central body (planet, star, etc.) due to gravity. The satellite is continuously 'falling' toward the central body, but its tangential velocity keeps it from getting closer — the result is a closed orbit. For circular orbits: gravity provides exactly the centripetal force, F_g = m·v^2/r. Newton (1687) explained the Moon's orbit, planetary orbits, and tides using the same gravitational law that explains apple-falling. Kepler's three laws (1609, 1619) had earlier described planetary orbital geometry; Newton derived them from universal gravitation. The International Space Station orbits Earth at altitude ~400 km, v ≈ 7.7 km/s, T ≈ 92 min. Geosynchronous satellites orbit at r ≈ 42,000 km with T = 24 hours, appearing stationary relative to Earth's surface. On Regents items, students apply F_g = F_c to find orbital parameters and use Kepler's third law.

questions.prompt: Net inward (downward) force at the top of a vertical circle, supplied by gravity and the normal/tension force.
questions.explanation: At the top of a vertical loop (e.g., a roller coaster, a ball on a string), the centripetal direction is downward (toward the center of the loop), so both gravity and the normal force (or tension) point downward and combine to supply the centripetal force: m·g + N = m·v^2/r. Solving: N = m·v^2/r - m·g. For the object to maintain contact with the track, N ≥ 0, so v^2 ≥ g·r, giving v_min = sqrt(g·r) at the top. At minimum speed, N = 0 and gravity alone supplies the centripetal force. On Regents items, this is the classic roller coaster loop problem: 'What is the minimum speed at the top to maintain contact?' The answer: v_min = sqrt(g·r). For a ball on a string, T + mg = mv^2/r at the top; if v_top < sqrt(gr), the string goes slack.

questions.prompt: Net inward (upward) force at the bottom of a vertical circle, where normal force is larger than weight.
questions.explanation: At the bottom of a vertical loop, the centripetal direction is upward (toward the center of the loop). Gravity points downward (away from center, providing -mg toward center), and the normal force points upward (toward center, providing +N). Newton's second law: N - m·g = m·v^2/r. Solving: N = m·g + m·v^2/r — the normal force exceeds the object's weight (the rider feels heavier, with apparent weight = N). For a roller coaster at the bottom of a vertical loop moving at v with loop radius r: the rider experiences an apparent weight N = m(g + v^2/r), which can be 2-3× their normal weight. On Regents items, students compute the normal force at the bottom from N = m·g + m·v^2/r, contrast with the top (where N = m·v^2/r - m·g), and interpret apparent weight in terms of the centripetal force requirement.

questions.prompt: Reading on a scale equal to the normal force, which differs from true weight during acceleration.
questions.explanation: Apparent weight is the reading on a scale on which a person stands, equal to the normal force the scale exerts on the person: W_apparent = N. In an inertial (non-accelerating) frame at rest, N = m·g (true weight). When the system accelerates: in an upward-accelerating elevator, N - m·g = m·a (Newton's second law), so N = m(g + a) > m·g — apparent weight increases. In a downward-accelerating elevator (a downward): N = m(g - a) < m·g. In free fall (a = g downward): N = 0 — apparent weightlessness. At the bottom of a vertical loop: N = m(g + v^2/r). At the top of a vertical loop: N = m(v^2/r - g). On Regents items, students compute apparent weight in elevators, in loops, and on banked turns, recognizing that apparent weight reflects the normal force, not the gravitational force (which is unchanged).

questions.prompt: Lowest speed at which a vertical-loop motion can be sustained without losing contact, v_min equals square root of g r at top of loop.
questions.explanation: Minimum speed in a vertical loop is the lowest speed at which the object can maintain contact with the track (or maintain tension in a string) at the top of the loop. At the top: gravity and normal force both point downward (toward center), so m·g + N = m·v^2/r. For minimum speed, N = 0 (just barely maintaining contact), giving v_min^2 = g·r, so v_min = sqrt(g·r). For a roller coaster loop of radius 10 m: v_min = sqrt(9.81 × 10) ≈ 9.9 m/s at the top. Below this speed, the car would lose contact with the track and follow a parabolic path. On Regents items, students compute v_min for various loop sizes, find the minimum height to enter the loop (using energy conservation: starting height must produce v_min at the top, so h_start ≥ 2.5·r above the bottom of the loop), and recognize that at minimum speed, gravity alone supplies the centripetal force.

questions.prompt: Variation in the normal force around a vertical circle, smallest at the top and largest at the bottom.
questions.explanation: The normal force varies continuously around a vertical circle. At the top: N = m·v^2/r - m·g (smallest, can be zero or negative meaning loss of contact). At the bottom: N = m·v^2/r + m·g (largest). At the sides (horizontal positions): N must supply the full centripetal force, but gravity is tangent to the circle (not radial), so N = m·v^2/r at those points (with gravity affecting the tangential acceleration, not the radial). Speed also varies due to energy conservation in a frictionless loop: v_bottom > v_top. Combining: v_bottom^2 = v_top^2 + 4·g·r (from PE = 2·g·r between top and bottom). So N_bottom = m·v_bottom^2/r + m·g = m·(v_top^2 + 4gr)/r + m·g = m·v_top^2/r + 5·m·g. At minimum, v_top^2 = g·r, so N_bottom = m·g·(1 + 5) = 6·m·g — riders feel 6× their weight at the bottom of a minimum-speed loop.

questions.prompt: Square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semimajor axis, T squared proportional to a cubed.
questions.explanation: Kepler's third law (Johannes Kepler, 1619) states that the square of an orbital period (T) is proportional to the cube of the orbit's semimajor axis (a): T^2 = (4·pi^2/G·M_central)·a^3 (Newton's derivation). For orbits around the same central body, T^2/a^3 = constant. The law works for all planets orbiting the Sun and was used by Kepler in 1619 to relate orbital periods and distances. Newton derived it from his universal law of gravitation in the 1687 Principia (gravity = centripetal force, with v = 2·pi·r/T for circular orbits). On Regents items, students use Kepler's third law to compare orbital periods of two satellites (T_1^2/T_2^2 = a_1^3/a_2^3), find unknown distance given period, or vice versa. The Moon orbits Earth at a ≈ 384,000 km, T ≈ 27.3 days. Jupiter orbits the Sun at a ≈ 5.2 AU, T ≈ 11.9 years.

questions.prompt: Closed orbital path shaped like an ellipse, with the central body at one focus (Kepler's first law).
questions.explanation: Kepler's first law (1609) states that planetary orbits are ellipses with the Sun at one focus. An ellipse is characterized by its semimajor axis (a, longest radius) and eccentricity (e, from 0 = circle to less than 1 = elongated ellipse). For Earth, e ≈ 0.017 (nearly circular). For comets, e can be close to 1 (highly elongated). For Mercury, e ≈ 0.206 (most elliptical of the planets). At perihelion (closest approach), the orbiting body moves fastest; at aphelion (farthest), slowest — Kepler's second law (1609) makes this quantitative (equal areas swept in equal times). Newton (Principia, 1687) derived all three Kepler laws from his inverse-square gravitational law, showing that any closed orbit under inverse-square attraction is an ellipse (circles being a special case with e = 0). On Regents items, students recognize that orbital speed varies along an ellipse and that the central body sits at one focus, not the center.

questions.prompt: Longest diameter of an elliptical orbit, twice the semimajor axis.
questions.explanation: The major axis of an ellipse is its longest diameter, equal to 2·a where a is the semimajor axis. For an elliptical orbit, the semimajor axis (a) is the average of the perihelion (closest) and aphelion (farthest) distances: a = (r_peri + r_apo)/2. The semimajor axis is the relevant length in Kepler's third law (T^2 ∝ a^3), not the actual orbital radius (which varies). For a circular orbit (special case of ellipse with e = 0), a equals the constant radius. On Regents items, students use a in Kepler's third law to relate periods and average orbital distances, and recognize that two orbits with different shapes but the same semimajor axis have the same period (T depends only on a, not on e). Earth's a = 1 AU = 1.496 × 10^11 m; Mars's a ≈ 1.524 AU, so by Kepler's third law T_Mars/T_Earth = (1.524)^1.5 ≈ 1.88 years.

questions.prompt: Time for a satellite to complete one full orbit, related to orbital radius by Kepler's third law.
questions.explanation: Orbital period (T) is the time for a satellite to complete one full orbit around its central body. From Kepler's third law: T = 2·pi·sqrt(a^3/G·M_central), where a is the semimajor axis and M_central is the mass of the central body. SI unit is the second. For Earth orbiting the Sun: T = 1 year = 3.16 × 10^7 s. For the Moon orbiting Earth: T = 27.3 days = 2.36 × 10^6 s. For the International Space Station (low Earth orbit, a ≈ 6.78 × 10^6 m): T ≈ 5560 s ≈ 92.7 min. For geosynchronous satellites: T = 24 hours, a ≈ 42,000 km. On Regents items, students compute T from a (and M_central), or compute a from a known T, and apply Kepler's third law to compare two orbits around the same central body: T_1^2/T_2^2 = a_1^3/a_2^3. The dependence on M_central means orbits around different bodies (Sun vs Earth) cannot be directly compared without including the mass.

questions.prompt: Force directed along the line connecting two objects, with magnitude depending only on their separation.
questions.explanation: A central force is a force whose direction always points along the line connecting two interacting objects, with magnitude depending only on their separation. Gravitational force (F_g = G·m_1·m_2/r^2) and electrostatic force (F = k·q_1·q_2/r^2) are the two foundational central forces in physics. Central forces conserve angular momentum (a key consequence is Kepler's second law, equal areas in equal times). Newton (1687) showed that an inverse-square central force produces closed elliptical orbits with the source at one focus. Other central forces (e.g., a spring connecting two masses) produce different orbital shapes. On Regents items, central forces are usually discussed implicitly: students apply F_g = G·m_1·m_2/r^2 along the line connecting the masses and use the centripetal-force condition for circular orbits. The 1/r^2 form of Newtonian gravity is what makes elliptical orbits stable and closed; small deviations would cause orbits to precess.

questions.prompt: Belief that an outward force pushes objects in circular motion, which is wrong in an inertial frame.
questions.explanation: The centrifugal misconception is the belief that an outward force pushes objects away from the center during circular motion. In an inertial (non-rotating) frame, this is false: the only forces on a circling object are real forces (gravity, normal, friction, tension, etc.) that combine to produce a net inward force (centripetal). The OUTWARD sensation a passenger feels in a turning car is due to their inertia (Newton's first law) — their body 'wants' to continue in a straight line tangent to the circle while the car turns inward, and the car door pushes them inward (toward the center). The 'centrifugal force' is a fictitious force that appears only in rotating reference frames (non-inertial). On Regents items, students must analyze circular motion in inertial frames using only real forces and identify the net inward force as centripetal. A common Regents distractor invokes 'centrifugal force' as a real outward force — this is wrong.

questions.prompt: Direction of velocity for an object in circular motion, always tangent to the circle.
questions.explanation: For an object in circular motion, velocity is ALWAYS tangent to the circle at every instant — perpendicular to the radius pointing toward the center. Even at constant speed, the velocity direction continuously changes (which is why circular motion involves acceleration). When a ball on a string is released, it flies off in the direction of velocity at the moment of release — i.e., tangent to the original circle, NOT radially outward (a common misconception). On Regents items, students must correctly identify the direction of velocity at any specified point on a circular path: tangent, not radial. A typical question shows an object on a circular track and asks 'If the string breaks at point X, in what direction does the object move?' The answer is tangentially from X, in the direction of motion at that instant. Galileo's law of inertia (Newton's first) explains this: with no force after release, the object moves in a straight line along its instantaneous velocity vector.

questions.prompt: Direction of acceleration for an object in uniform circular motion, always radially inward (centripetal).
questions.explanation: For an object in uniform circular motion (constant speed), acceleration is ALWAYS directed radially inward (toward the center of the circle) — this is centripetal acceleration. The direction is perpendicular to the instantaneous velocity (which is tangential). At constant speed, there is no tangential acceleration; all acceleration is centripetal. If speed is increasing or decreasing (non-uniform circular motion), there is a tangential acceleration component in addition to the centripetal component, but the centripetal component remains radial. On Regents items, students must identify acceleration as pointing toward the center, NOT in the direction of motion. A common error is drawing acceleration along the velocity vector (which would be tangential). For circular motion, velocity tangential and acceleration radial are always perpendicular. Newton's second law gives F_net = m·a_c radially inward.

questions.prompt: Direction of net force on an object in circular motion, always radially inward toward the center.
questions.explanation: For an object in circular motion at constant speed, the net force is directed radially inward (toward the center of the circle). By Newton's second law: F_net = m·a_c radially inward, where a_c = v^2/r. This net force is supplied by the vector sum of real forces (gravity, tension, normal, friction, etc.) on the object — there is no separate 'centripetal force' arrow on a free-body diagram. For a car turning on a flat road, the net inward force is friction. For a ball on a string in a horizontal circle, the net force is the horizontal component of tension. For a planet orbiting the Sun, the net force is gravity. On Regents items, students draw a complete free-body diagram with all real forces, then identify that the SUM of those forces points toward the circle's center (and equals m·v^2/r). The 'centripetal' label describes the direction (inward) of the net force, not a new force type.

questions.prompt: Diagram for circular motion in which the sum of forces must point toward the center, supplying centripetal force.
questions.explanation: A free-body diagram for circular motion shows all real forces acting on the object: gravity, normal force, tension, friction, applied force, etc. The KEY constraint is that the vector sum of these real forces must point toward the center of the circle and have magnitude m·v^2/r — this provides the centripetal acceleration. Common mistake: drawing an additional 'centripetal force' arrow as if it were a separate, distinct force; it is NOT — it is the net result of the other forces already on the diagram. For a car on a banked turn: gravity (down) and normal force (perpendicular to road surface) combine to produce a net horizontal inward component supplying centripetal force, with no friction needed if the bank angle matches the speed. For an object on a vertical loop: tension/normal and gravity combine differently at top and bottom. On Regents items, students draw the FBD, identify each force, then show that the vector sum is radial-inward with magnitude m·v^2/r.

questions.prompt: Fundamental property of matter, measured in coulombs, that gives rise to electric force.
questions.explanation: Electric charge (q) is a fundamental property of matter that produces electric force. SI unit is the coulomb (C). Charge comes in two types — positive and negative — and like charges repel while unlike charges attract. Protons carry positive charge (+e = +1.60 × 10^-19 C); electrons carry negative charge (-e). Neutral matter contains equal numbers of protons and electrons. Charge can be transferred between objects (by friction, contact, or induction) but the total charge in a closed system is conserved. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list the elementary charge e = 1.60 × 10^-19 C and Coulomb's constant k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m^2/C^2. On Regents items, students convert between coulombs and elementary charges (Q = n·e, where n is the number of excess electrons or protons), compute forces using Coulomb's law, and apply conservation of charge to charge-transfer problems. Benjamin Franklin established the positive/negative convention in the 1750s.

questions.prompt: Total electric charge in a closed system is constant, with charge neither created nor destroyed.
questions.explanation: Conservation of charge is a fundamental physical law: the total electric charge in any closed system is constant. Charge can be transferred between objects (e.g., by rubbing, contact, or induction), but the total charge of all objects in the system before equals the total after. When two identical metal spheres carrying charges of +6 C and -2 C are brought into contact, they share charge equally: each ends up with (+6 + (-2))/2 = +2 C, and the total +4 C is unchanged. In nuclear reactions, the sum of charges on the reactants equals the sum on the products (e.g., beta decay: neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino, with charges 0 → +1 + (-1) + 0 = 0). On Regents items, students apply conservation of charge to charge-transfer problems (touching spheres, induction), nuclear decay equations, and electric circuits (current at any node is conserved — Kirchhoff's current law).

questions.prompt: Smallest unit of free electric charge, e equals 1.60 × 10 to the negative 19 coulombs, carried by a single proton or electron.
questions.explanation: The elementary charge (e) is the magnitude of charge on a single proton (+e) or electron (-e): e = 1.60 × 10^-19 C (NYS Physics Reference Tables). It is the smallest unit of free charge — all observable charges in nature are integer multiples of e (charge quantization). Robert Millikan first measured e precisely in 1909 using his oil-drop experiment (Nobel Prize 1923). One coulomb corresponds to 6.25 × 10^18 elementary charges. A neutral atom has zero net charge (equal protons and electrons); ions have non-zero charge equal to ±n·e where n is the number of electrons gained or lost. Note: quarks have fractional charges (±e/3 or ±2e/3), but they are never observed in isolation, so free charges remain integer multiples of e. On Regents items, students convert charge values to numbers of elementary charges (n = Q/e), recognize that charge is always quantized, and use e in calculating ion charges and currents.

questions.prompt: Electrostatic force between two point charges is k q_1 q_2 over r squared, an inverse-square law (Coulomb 1785).
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law (Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, 1785) gives the electrostatic force between two point charges: F_e = k·|q_1·q_2|/r^2, where k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m^2/C^2 is Coulomb's constant, q_1 and q_2 are the charges (in coulombs), and r is the distance between them. The force is along the line connecting the charges; like charges repel (positive F outward), unlike charges attract (positive F inward). The NYS Physics Reference Tables provide both this formula and k. Coulomb's law has the same inverse-square form as Newton's law of gravitation (1687), but it is approximately 10^36 times stronger between an electron and proton than gravity between the same particles. On Regents items, students compute force magnitudes from Coulomb's law, identify direction (attractive or repulsive), and apply superposition: when multiple charges are present, the net force on any one is the vector sum of forces from each other charge.

questions.prompt: Force one charged object exerts on another, attractive for unlike charges and repulsive for like charges.
questions.explanation: Electric force (F_e) is the force between charged objects, given by Coulomb's law: F_e = k·|q_1·q_2|/r^2. Like charges (both positive or both negative) repel each other; unlike charges (one positive, one negative) attract. The force lies along the line connecting the charges. The magnitude follows the inverse-square law: doubling the distance reduces the force by a factor of 4. Force is also proportional to the product of the charges: doubling either charge doubles the force. The NYS Reference Tables list F_e = k·q_1·q_2/r^2 (with k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m^2/C^2). On Regents items, students determine direction (attractive or repulsive) from charge signs, compute magnitude from Coulomb's law, and apply superposition to find net force from multiple charges. Two charges 1 C apart experience F_e = 8.99 × 10^9 N — coulombs are huge units; typical charges are microcoulombs (1 μC = 10^-6 C) or smaller.

questions.prompt: Decrease of electric force as 1 over r squared with distance, identical in form to Newton's gravitational law.
questions.explanation: The inverse-square relationship in Coulomb's law (F_e ∝ 1/r^2) parallels Newton's law of gravitation (F_g ∝ 1/r^2) — both are inverse-square central forces. The geometric origin: an electric field from a point charge spreads uniformly in 3D space, with total flux constant but flux density (proportional to field strength) decreasing as 1/(4·pi·r^2). The 1/r^2 form also applies to gravitational fields, light intensity from point sources, and any radiation spreading uniformly from a point. Doubling the distance reduces F_e (and F_g) by a factor of 4. Tripling reduces by a factor of 9. On Regents items, students recognize this scaling: 'If the distance between two charges is halved, the force changes by what factor?' Answer: ×4. The inverse-square form ensures stable closed orbits (planetary motion) and the existence of bound atoms (electron-proton attraction in hydrogen, which led to Bohr's 1913 atomic model).

questions.prompt: Small positive charge used to probe an electric field without disturbing the source charges.
questions.explanation: A test charge (q_0) is a small, hypothetical positive charge introduced into an electric field to measure the field's strength and direction at that point. By convention, the test charge is positive; the electric field direction is defined as the direction of force on a positive test charge: E = F/q_0. The test charge must be small enough that it does not measurably affect the source charge distribution producing the field (otherwise it would change the very field being measured). In real experiments, test charges are idealized; field measurements use other techniques (electrometers, voltage probes). On Regents items, students use the concept of a test charge to determine field direction: 'In which direction would a positive test charge experience force?' The answer is the field direction at that point. Field lines are visualized as the paths a positive test charge would follow if released.

questions.prompt: Principle that net electric force or field is the vector sum of contributions from each individual source.
questions.explanation: The superposition principle states that the net electric force (or net electric field) on any charge is the vector sum of forces (or fields) from each individual source charge, calculated as if each were the only source present. Electric forces and fields obey linear superposition because Coulomb's law is linear in charge. For three charges (+q at A, -q at B, +q at C), the force on a fourth charge q' is F = F_A→q' + F_B→q' + F_C→q' — a vector sum requiring decomposition into x and y components in 2D. On Regents items, students apply superposition to find net force on a charge in a configuration: compute the magnitude of each pairwise force from Coulomb's law, identify direction (attractive or repulsive), decompose into components, then sum components separately. The superposition principle is a cornerstone of classical electromagnetism and underlies all electric-circuit and field analyses.

questions.prompt: Region around a charged object where another charge experiences electric force, E equals F over q.
questions.explanation: An electric field (E) is the region around a charged object in which another charged object experiences electric force. The field strength is defined as the force per unit charge on a small positive test charge: E = F/q_0, with SI units of N/C (equivalent to V/m). The NYS Reference Tables list E = F/q. For a point charge: E = k·Q/r^2 at distance r from the source charge Q. Electric fields are vectors: the direction is the direction of force on a positive test charge (away from positive source charges, toward negative source charges). The field concept (introduced by Michael Faraday in the early 1830s, formalized by Maxwell in 1864) replaced the notion of action-at-a-distance with the idea that charges create fields, and fields exert forces on other charges. On Regents items, students compute E from Coulomb's law, find force on a charge from F = q·E, and add fields by vector superposition.

questions.prompt: Imaginary line tangent to the electric field vector at every point, used to visualize field structure.
questions.explanation: An electric field line is an imaginary line drawn so that the tangent to the line at any point gives the direction of the electric field at that point. Field lines are visualization tools for the spatial structure of electric fields. Key properties: (1) Field lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges (or extend to infinity); (2) Field lines never cross (the field has a unique direction at every point); (3) The density of field lines is proportional to field strength (closely packed = strong field; spread out = weak field); (4) Field lines are perpendicular to conductor surfaces at the surface. For a single positive point charge, field lines radiate outward radially; for a single negative charge, they converge inward. For a dipole (one + and one -), they curve from + to -. Michael Faraday introduced field lines in the early 1830s as a way to visualize electromagnetic fields without invoking action-at-a-distance. On Regents items, students sketch field lines for various charge configurations and interpret given diagrams.

questions.prompt: Region where the electric field has the same magnitude and direction at every point, produced between parallel plates.
questions.explanation: A uniform electric field has the same magnitude and direction at every point in a region. The classic example is the region between two large, oppositely charged parallel plates (a parallel-plate capacitor) — far from the plate edges, the field is uniform: E = V/d, where V is the potential difference between the plates and d is the plate separation. Field lines in a uniform field are straight, parallel, and equally spaced, pointing from the positive plate to the negative plate. A charge q placed in a uniform field experiences a constant force F = q·E (independent of position within the uniform region). This makes uniform-field problems mathematically simple: a charged particle starting at rest accelerates uniformly (constant acceleration a = q·E/m), reaching speed v after distance d via v^2 = 2·a·d. On Regents items, students compute E from V/d, find force on charges, and analyze motion of charges (mass spectrometer, oscilloscope deflection plates).

questions.prompt: Radially symmetric electric field around a single charge, E equals k Q over r squared.
questions.explanation: The electric field around a single isolated point charge is radially symmetric and given by E = k·Q/r^2, where Q is the source charge, r is the distance from the source, and k = 8.99 × 10^9 N·m^2/C^2 is Coulomb's constant. The NYS Reference Tables list this formula. Field direction is radial — outward for positive Q, inward for negative Q. Magnitude decreases with the inverse square of distance: at twice r, E is one-quarter; at three times r, E is one-ninth. For multiple point charges, the net field at any location is the vector sum of fields from each individual point charge (superposition). On Regents items, students compute E at specified distances from a point charge, find the field at a midpoint between two charges (using superposition), and recognize that doubling the distance reduces the field by a factor of 4. The point-charge field is the building block for all charge-distribution field calculations.

questions.prompt: Energy stored due to position of a charge in an electric field, PE_E equals k q_1 q_2 over r for two point charges.
questions.explanation: Electric potential energy (PE_E) is the energy stored in a configuration of charges due to their positions in each other's electric fields. For two point charges separated by distance r: PE_E = k·q_1·q_2/r (positive for like charges, negative for unlike). Note the 1/r dependence — distinct from Coulomb force's 1/r^2. PE_E represents the work required to assemble the charge configuration from infinitely far apart. For charges with opposite signs (attractive force), PE_E is negative — the system is 'bound' (assembled state has lower energy than separated). For like charges (repulsive), PE_E is positive — work must be done to bring them together. On Regents items, students compute PE_E for point-charge configurations, apply conservation of energy (KE + PE_E = constant) for charges moving in fields, and convert between PE_E and KE for a charge accelerating from rest under electric force. Related to electric potential by PE_E = q·V.

questions.prompt: Electric potential energy per unit charge, V equals PE over q, measured in volts.
questions.explanation: Electric potential (V) is the electric potential energy per unit charge: V = PE_E/q. SI unit is the volt (V), defined as 1 joule per coulomb. The NYS Reference Tables list V = W/q (potential is also the work done per unit charge to bring a test charge from infinity to the point). For a point charge at the origin, the potential at distance r is V = k·Q/r (positive for Q > 0, negative for Q < 0). Note the 1/r dependence — different from the 1/r^2 of field and force. Potential is a scalar (not a vector), making it easier to compute for complex configurations: just add scalar contributions from each source charge. The difference in potential between two points is the potential difference V_AB = V_A - V_B, measured in volts. On Regents items, students compute V from k·Q/r, sum scalar potentials from multiple charges (superposition), and use V = E·d for uniform fields.

questions.prompt: Potential difference between two points, V equals W over q, equal to energy per unit charge transferred between them.
questions.explanation: Voltage (V), or potential difference, is the electric potential difference between two points in space or in a circuit: V_AB = V_A - V_B. SI unit is the volt (V), defined as 1 joule per coulomb. Voltage represents the energy per unit charge transferred when a charge moves from one point to the other: W = q·V. The NYS Reference Tables list V = W/q. A 1.5 V battery delivers 1.5 J of energy per coulomb of charge that flows through it. Voltage is the 'electric pressure' that drives current through a circuit, analogous to pressure difference driving fluid flow. Higher voltage = more energy per unit charge available to do work. On Regents items, voltage appears in Coulomb's law derivative contexts (V from k·Q/r), in circuit problems (V = I·R, Ohm's law, Unit 7), in capacitor and field problems (E = V/d for parallel plates), and in energy calculations (W = q·V for a charge moving through a potential difference).

questions.prompt: Surface in space on which electric potential is constant, perpendicular to electric field lines.
questions.explanation: An equipotential surface is a surface in space on which the electric potential V is constant. No work is required to move a charge along an equipotential surface (since W = q·ΔV = 0 for ΔV = 0). Equipotentials are always perpendicular to electric field lines at every point (the field is the negative gradient of potential, so it points in the direction of steepest potential decrease). For a single positive point charge, equipotentials are concentric spheres centered on the charge. For a parallel-plate capacitor, equipotentials are parallel planes perpendicular to the field lines. For irregular charge distributions, equipotentials curve to remain perpendicular to field lines everywhere. On Regents items, students recognize that conductors are equipotential surfaces in equilibrium (since charges in a conductor are free to move, they redistribute until V is constant throughout), and understand that field lines must be perpendicular to a conductor's surface at the surface.

questions.prompt: Energy required to move a unit charge between two points, equal to the potential difference in volts.
questions.explanation: Work per unit charge required to move a charge between two points is the potential difference: W/q = V_AB. This is the operational definition of voltage. SI unit: 1 V = 1 J/C. For a 12 V battery, moving 1 C of charge from the negative terminal to the positive terminal (through an external circuit) requires 12 J of work done against the field. Conversely, the battery delivers 12 J of energy per coulomb of charge passing through it. On Regents items, students compute energy or work from W = q·V, find the speed of a charge accelerated through a potential difference (use conservation of energy: q·V = (1/2)m·v^2, giving v = sqrt(2qV/m)), and analyze capacitor energy storage. The 1 eV (electron volt) energy unit is defined as the kinetic energy gained by one electron accelerated through 1 V: 1 eV = e·1 V = 1.60 × 10^-19 J — a convenient unit for atomic and subatomic physics.

questions.prompt: Energy unit equal to the kinetic energy of one electron accelerated through one volt, 1 eV equals 1.60 × 10 to the negative 19 joules.
questions.explanation: An electron volt (eV) is a non-SI energy unit defined as the kinetic energy gained by an electron (charge e) accelerated through a potential difference of 1 V: 1 eV = e·(1 V) = 1.60 × 10^-19 J. The NYS Reference Tables provide this conversion. The eV is convenient for atomic, molecular, and nuclear energy scales: hydrogen ground state binding energy = 13.6 eV; visible light photons have energies 1.6-3.3 eV; X-ray photons are keV (10^3 eV); nuclear binding energies are MeV (10^6 eV); particle physics uses GeV (10^9 eV) and TeV (10^12 eV). Atomic and nuclear physics texts and Regents physics problems use eV throughout. On Regents items, students convert between eV and joules (multiply by 1.60 × 10^-19) and compute photon energies (E = h·f in joules, then divide by 1.6 × 10^-19 to get eV). The Large Hadron Collider's beam energy is ~7 TeV per particle.

questions.prompt: Transfer of charge between a charged object and an uncharged conductor when they touch, sharing charge proportionally.
questions.explanation: Charging by contact (charging by conduction) occurs when a charged object touches a neutral conductor — some of the excess charge transfers from one to the other, leaving both with charge of the same sign. The amount transferred depends on the relative capacitances; for two identical metal spheres, the charge is shared equally. If a +10 nC sphere touches an identical neutral sphere, both end up at +5 nC. Conservation of charge ensures the total is conserved. The two objects must be conductors (or partial conductors) for significant charge transfer; insulators do not share charge readily. On Regents items, students apply conservation of charge to contact problems (e.g., two charged spheres in succession share charge), recognize that the contacted body ends up with the same sign of charge, and contrast this with charging by induction (which uses no contact).

questions.prompt: Connection of a conductor to Earth, which can transfer charge to or from the very large reservoir.
questions.explanation: Grounding is the connection of a conductor to Earth (or to a very large reservoir of charge), allowing excess charge to flow to or from the conductor to neutralize it. Earth, being so large, can absorb or supply any practical amount of charge without significantly changing its own potential. Grounding is used in: (1) Lightning rods (channel lightning current safely to Earth); (2) Electrical safety (the third prong of a US three-prong plug connects the appliance casing to ground, so any short circuit causes the breaker to trip rather than shocking the user); (3) Charging by induction (grounding removes induced like charges, leaving the conductor with opposite net charge after the ground is removed). The ground symbol in circuit diagrams is three downward-pointing horizontal lines. On Regents items, students apply grounding in induction problems and recognize its role in electrical safety. Benjamin Franklin's 1752 kite experiment led to lightning rods using grounding.

questions.prompt: Instrument detecting the presence and sign of static electric charge via repulsion of charged metal leaves.
questions.explanation: An electroscope is a sensitive instrument for detecting and measuring static electric charge. The classic design consists of two thin metal foil leaves (usually gold) hanging from a metal rod inside a glass jar (to prevent air currents from disturbing them). The metal terminal extends through the jar's top. When charge is added to the terminal (by touching it with a charged object or by induction), the charge spreads to the leaves; the leaves carry like charges and repel each other, spreading apart. The angle of spread is proportional to the magnitude of charge — more charge means wider spread. An electroscope can determine the sign of an unknown charge: if a known-sign charged object is brought near a leaves-spread electroscope, the spread increases for same-sign and decreases (or reverses) for opposite-sign charge. On Regents items, students explain how an electroscope works, predict leaf behavior in induction and contact charging scenarios, and use it to distinguish charge signs.

questions.prompt: Rate of flow of charge past a point in a circuit, I equals Q over t, measured in amperes.
questions.explanation: Electric current (I) is the rate at which electric charge flows past a given point in a circuit: I = Q/t. SI unit is the ampere (A), defined as 1 coulomb per second. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list I = ΔQ/Δt. Conventional current direction is the direction positive charges would flow (from + to - terminal in the external circuit) — opposite to the direction of electron flow. In most conductors (metals), the charge carriers are actually electrons moving the other way, but conventional current is the standardized historical convention (Benjamin Franklin's choice in the 1750s, before electron discovery in 1897). A 2-A current means 2 coulombs of charge pass per second, or 1.25 × 10^19 electrons. On Regents items, students compute current from Q/t, find total charge from I·t, count electrons (n = Q/e), and apply Kirchhoff's current law (current is conserved at junctions). Current is a scalar (despite having direction) and is the same throughout a series circuit.

questions.prompt: Opposition to current flow in a conductor, R equals V over I, measured in ohms.
questions.explanation: Resistance (R) is a conductor's opposition to electric current. From Ohm's law (Georg Ohm, 1827): R = V/I, with SI unit the ohm (Ω), defined as 1 volt per ampere. The NYS Reference Tables list R = V/I and L·rho/A (where rho is resistivity, L is length, A is cross-sectional area). Resistance increases with conductor length, decreases with cross-sectional area, and depends on material (resistivity rho, NYS table values: copper 1.72 × 10^-8 Ω·m, aluminum 2.82 × 10^-8 Ω·m, silver 1.59 × 10^-8 Ω·m — silver is the best conductor, but copper is much cheaper). Temperature also affects R (most metals increase resistance with temperature). On Regents items, students compute R from V/I, from rho·L/A, find equivalent R in series and parallel circuits, and recognize that lower R means easier current flow. An open circuit has R = infinity (no current); a short circuit has R ≈ 0 (very large current).

questions.prompt: Voltage between two points in a circuit, equal to energy per unit charge transferred between them.
questions.explanation: Potential difference (V), or voltage, between two points in a circuit is the energy per unit charge that would be transferred when a charge moves between them: V = W/Q. SI unit is the volt (V), defined as 1 joule per coulomb. The NYS Reference Tables list V = W/Q and V = I·R (Ohm's law). A battery is a source of potential difference (electromotive force, EMF), supplying energy per coulomb that drives current through the external circuit. In a series circuit, potential differences across components add to the source voltage (Kirchhoff's voltage law). In a parallel circuit, every branch has the same potential difference. On Regents items, students compute V from W/Q, from I·R, from energy considerations, and apply Kirchhoff's laws to multi-loop circuits. The choice of 'ground' (zero reference) is arbitrary; only differences matter physically.

questions.prompt: Rate at which an electrical device transfers energy, P equals I times V, measured in watts.
questions.explanation: Power (P) is the rate at which electrical energy is transferred or converted: P = I·V (NYS Reference Tables). Using Ohm's law, this can be rewritten as P = I^2·R or P = V^2/R. SI unit is the watt (W), defined as 1 joule per second. A 100-W incandescent bulb consumes 100 J of electrical energy per second; a 1500-W hair dryer consumes 1500 J/s. The power rating on appliances assumes a specific operating voltage (typically 120 V in the US, 240 V in many other countries). A device drawing 0.83 A at 120 V dissipates P = 100 W. On Regents items, students compute power three ways (P = IV, P = I^2R, P = V^2/R), find energy consumption W = P·t, and analyze circuits where multiple devices share a power budget. Higher-wattage devices are not necessarily 'larger' but transfer more energy per second; LED bulbs transfer the same useful light energy as incandescents at a much lower power rating because they waste less as heat.

questions.prompt: Total electrical energy used by a device over time, W equals P times t, measured in joules or kilowatt-hours.
questions.explanation: Energy consumption is the total electrical energy used by a device: W = P·t. SI unit is the joule (J); the practical unit for billing is the kilowatt-hour (kWh = 3.6 × 10^6 J). A 1000-W device running for 1 hour consumes 1 kWh; at $0.12/kWh, that's about 12 cents. On a Regents-relevant scale: a 100-W bulb on for 8 hours consumes 800 W·h = 0.8 kWh. On Regents items, students compute total energy from W = P·t (or W = I·V·t, W = I^2·R·t, W = V^2·t/R), convert between joules and kWh, and analyze cost or efficiency comparisons (LED vs incandescent: both produce ~800 lumens, but LED uses ~10 W vs incandescent's 60 W — over 1000 hours, energy savings are 50 kWh ≈ $6). Energy conservation requires that all electrical energy delivered to a resistor is converted to heat (Joule heating, Q = I^2·R·t — first quantified by James Joule in 1840s).

questions.prompt: Current through an ohmic resistor is directly proportional to voltage across it, V equals I times R (Ohm 1827).
questions.explanation: Ohm's law (Georg Ohm, 1827) states that for an ohmic resistor, the current flowing through is directly proportional to the voltage across it: V = I·R, where R (resistance) is a constant for the material at fixed temperature. The NYS Physics Reference Tables list V = I·R and the rearrangements I = V/R, R = V/I. Graphically, an I-V curve for an ohmic resistor is a straight line through the origin with slope 1/R. Non-ohmic devices (e.g., diodes, transistors, incandescent filaments at high current) have non-linear I-V curves. On Regents items, students apply Ohm's law to compute any one of V, I, R given the other two; combined with Kirchhoff's laws, Ohm's law solves any DC circuit. Ohm's discovery was initially controversial (some physicists thought current should be proportional to V^2 or other powers), but careful experiments confirmed the linear relationship for metallic conductors.

questions.prompt: Single resistor that would draw the same current as a network of resistors at the same voltage.
questions.explanation: Equivalent resistance (R_eq) is the single resistance value that would behave identically to a network of resistors at the terminals: V/I_total at the terminals = R_eq. For resistors in SERIES (end-to-end): R_eq = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + ... (current is the same through each; voltages add). For resistors in PARALLEL (side-by-side, same two endpoints): 1/R_eq = 1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + 1/R_3 + ... (voltages are equal; currents add). For two resistors in parallel: R_eq = R_1·R_2/(R_1 + R_2) (product over sum). Adding a resistor in series increases R_eq; adding one in parallel decreases R_eq. On Regents items, students compute R_eq for series, parallel, and mixed configurations by working step-by-step from the innermost combinations outward, then apply Ohm's law to the entire circuit using R_eq.

questions.prompt: Instrument measuring potential difference between two points, connected in parallel with high internal resistance.
questions.explanation: A voltmeter measures the potential difference (voltage) between two points in a circuit. Connection: a voltmeter is connected in PARALLEL with the component (across both endpoints). To minimize disturbance of the circuit, an ideal voltmeter has infinite internal resistance — so essentially no current flows through it, leaving the circuit's operation unchanged. Real voltmeters have very high (but finite) input impedance, typically 10 MΩ or higher, sufficient for most applications. On a circuit diagram, a voltmeter is drawn as a circle with V inside. On Regents items, students recognize: voltmeter goes in parallel with the device being measured; voltmeter readings should not significantly change the circuit; an open-circuit voltmeter (no actual connection) reads zero; a short-circuit voltmeter (wire connecting both leads) reads zero. Contrast with ammeters (which go in series and have very low resistance).

questions.prompt: Instrument measuring current at a point in a circuit, connected in series with very low internal resistance.
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures the current flowing through a point in a circuit. Connection: an ammeter is inserted in SERIES at the location where current is to be measured (the wire must be broken and the ammeter inserted in the gap). To minimize disturbance, an ideal ammeter has zero internal resistance — so it doesn't add resistance to the circuit. Real ammeters have very low (but non-zero) internal resistance, typically a fraction of an ohm. On a circuit diagram, an ammeter is drawn as a circle with A inside. WARNING: connecting an ammeter in PARALLEL with a voltage source creates a near-short-circuit and can damage the meter. On Regents items, students recognize: ammeter goes in series with the device being measured; ammeter should not add significant resistance; correct symbol is A in a circle; for measuring current in a series circuit, the ammeter can go anywhere along the loop (same current everywhere).

questions.prompt: Standardized graphical representation of an electrical component, used in schematic diagrams.
questions.explanation: Circuit symbols are standardized graphical representations of electrical components used in schematic diagrams. Common symbols (NYS Reference Tables): battery/cell — two parallel lines (long line is +, short is -); resistor — zigzag (US convention) or rectangle (international IEC); switch — line with break and label SW; ammeter — circle with A; voltmeter — circle with V; bulb — circle with X inside or a symbol resembling a small bulb; capacitor — two parallel lines (one curved); ground — three downward-pointing horizontal lines; wire — straight line (junctions marked with a dot, crossings without dots indicate no connection). On Regents items, students draw and interpret circuit diagrams using these symbols, identify whether components are in series or parallel from the topology, and convert between schematic diagrams and physical circuit descriptions. Standardized symbols allow physicists and engineers worldwide to communicate circuit designs unambiguously.

questions.prompt: Defining property of a series circuit, where the rate of charge flow is identical through every component.
questions.explanation: Same current through every component is the defining property of a series circuit. By conservation of charge, the current that flows into one component must equal the current flowing out of it; in a series circuit with no branching, this means the same current flows through the entire loop. This rule applies regardless of how the components differ: a 100 Ω resistor and a 5 Ω resistor in series have the same current through them, just different voltages (V = I·R) and different powers (P = I^2·R, with the larger R consuming more power for the same I). On Regents items, students use 'same current in series' as their entry point: find total R_eq, compute I = V/R_eq, then use I in V = I·R or P = I^2·R for each individual resistor. A common Regents trap: assuming different currents in series components — this is incorrect.

questions.prompt: Decrease in potential across a resistor due to current flow, V equals I times R.
questions.explanation: Voltage drop is the decrease in electric potential as current flows through a resistor, given by Ohm's law: V_drop = I·R. In a series circuit, the sum of voltage drops across all resistors equals the source voltage (battery EMF): V_battery = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + ... = I·R_1 + I·R_2 + ... = I·R_eq. This is Kirchhoff's voltage law (Gustav Kirchhoff, 1845): the algebraic sum of all voltages around any closed loop is zero. A 12-V battery with three series resistors of 1 Ω, 2 Ω, 3 Ω (R_eq = 6 Ω, I = 2 A) has voltage drops 2 V, 4 V, 6 V respectively (summing to 12 V). On Regents items, students compute voltage drops for each component using V = I·R after finding total current, verify Kirchhoff's voltage law, and recognize that the largest resistor in a series circuit drops the most voltage and dissipates the most power.

questions.prompt: Equivalent resistance of a series circuit, R_total equals R_1 plus R_2 plus R_3 plus ellipsis.
questions.explanation: Total resistance in a series circuit is the simple algebraic sum of individual resistances: R_total = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + ... The NYS Reference Tables list this rule. Adding resistors in series always increases the total resistance, decreasing the current the source can drive. Three 10-Ω resistors in series give R_total = 30 Ω; with a 12-V battery, the current is I = V/R_total = 12/30 = 0.4 A. Common applications: voltage dividers (two resistors in series produce intermediate voltages); current limiting (a series resistor limits the maximum current through a sensitive component); LED protection (a resistor in series with an LED limits current to safe levels). On Regents items, students compute R_total for series networks, then use it to find total current from V_source/R_total. A common error: treating series total resistance like parallel (1/R sum) — the rule for series is simple addition.

questions.prompt: Series circuit with a break (e.g., a burned-out component) that prevents current flow, giving R equal to infinity.
questions.explanation: An open circuit is a circuit with a break (gap, broken wire, burned-out component, or open switch) that prevents the flow of current. Effectively R = infinity at the break. In a series circuit, a single open anywhere stops current everywhere in the loop. This is why old-style Christmas lights were notorious: one burned-out bulb darkened the entire string. Modern bulbs include shunting elements that maintain conductivity when the filament breaks. Open circuits are intentional in switches (the open position interrupts the circuit). On Regents items, students recognize that an open circuit produces zero current and voltmeter readings across the gap equal to the source voltage (all of V_source 'drops' across the open). When troubleshooting, an open in series with other components stops all current; an open in parallel with other components removes that branch but doesn't stop overall current.

questions.prompt: Current through one path of a parallel circuit, I_i equals V over R_i for each branch.
questions.explanation: Branch current is the current through a single path (branch) of a parallel circuit. Since each branch has the same voltage V across it (equal to the source voltage if directly across the battery), the current through branch i is I_i = V/R_i. Branches with smaller resistance carry more current. Total current from the source is the sum: I_total = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + ... (Kirchhoff's current law). For a 12-V source with three parallel branches of 2 Ω, 3 Ω, 6 Ω: each has V = 12 V across it; branch currents are 6 A, 4 A, 2 A; total = 12 A; R_eq = 12/12 = 1 Ω (also computable as 1/R_eq = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6 = 1). On Regents items, students compute branch currents from V/R_i, sum them to verify total current, and recognize that the smallest-resistance branch carries the largest current.

questions.prompt: Defining property of a parallel circuit, where every branch has the identical potential difference.
questions.explanation: Same voltage across every branch is the defining property of a parallel circuit.
